example: Before hospitalization for gallstone surgery: doctor consultation (₹1,000), ultrasound (₹2,500), blood tests (₹3,000), and pre-surgical medications (₹1,500) over the 30 days before admission = ₹8,000. Your policy covers pre-hospitalization for 30 days — ₹8,000 is reimbursed.

definition: A reduction applied to the entire claim amount when the policyholder chooses a room category higher than the policy's room rent limit. The deduction ratio is: (policy room rent limit / actual room rent chosen). This ratio applies not just to room charges but to ALL associated expenses — doctor fees, nursing, consumables.
example: Policy room rent limit: ₹5,000/day. You choose a ₹10,000/day room. Deduction ratio: 50%. Total bill: ₹3 lakh. Insurer pays only 50% = ₹1.5 lakh. You pay ₹1.5 lakh. The proportional deduction on a ₹3 lakh bill far exceeds the ₹5,000/day room difference.

definition: A cap on the insurer's liability for specific diseases or procedures, regardless of the overall sum insured. Common disease-wise sub-limits apply to cataract, knee replacement, hernia, appendectomy, and maternity. Expressed as a fixed rupee amount or percentage of sum insured.
example: Your ₹10 lakh policy has disease-wise sub-limits: cataract ₹50,000 per eye, hernia ₹75,000, tonsils ₹30,000. If cataract surgery costs ₹80,000, the insurer pays only ₹50,000. You pay ₹30,000 even though your overall SI has ₹10 lakh available.

definition: A share of the insurer's profits distributed to participating (with-profits) policyholders. Declared annually as a rate per ₹1,000 of sum assured. Once declared, the bonus vests with the policyholder and cannot be taken back. Three types: reversionary (annual), terminal (one-time at maturity/death), and interim (between valuations).
example: LIC declares a bonus of ₹48 per ₹1,000 SA on Jeevan Anand in 2024-25. For a ₹10 lakh SA policy, annual bonus = ₹48 × 1,000 = ₹48,000. Over 20 years at this rate, total bonus = ₹9.6 lakh — nearly doubling the maturity value.

definition: The most common type of bonus in traditional life insurance plans, declared annually as a percentage of sum assured (simple reversionary) or of sum assured plus existing bonuses (compound reversionary). Once declared and vested, it is guaranteed and payable at maturity or death. Rates vary by insurer and policy type.
example: Simple reversionary bonus: ₹48/1000 SA declared on a ₹10 lakh policy = ₹48,000/year. After 20 years, total bonus = ₹9.6 lakh. Compound reversionary bonus: declared on SA + existing bonus, creating a compounding effect — total after 20 years would be higher.

definition: Bonuses that have been declared and are guaranteed to the policyholder, forming part of the policy's value. Once a bonus vests, it cannot be withdrawn by the insurer. Vested bonuses are payable on maturity or death. Even if the policy is surrendered, vested bonuses (at a reduced rate) form part of the surrender value.
example: Your LIC Jeevan Labh policy has accumulated vested bonuses of ₹3.5 lakh over 10 years on a ₹10 lakh SA. If you surrender the policy, the surrender value includes a portion of these vested bonuses. If you continue to maturity, you receive the full ₹3.5 lakh plus future bonuses.

definition: An additional bonus or addition to the policy value paid to policyholders who maintain their policy for a specified minimum period, typically 10-15 years. It rewards long-term persistence and is usually a one-time or periodic addition. Common in ULIPs and some traditional plans.
example: Your ULIP policy adds a loyalty bonus of 2% of fund value after completing 10 years. With a fund value of ₹8 lakh, the loyalty bonus = ₹16,000 added to your fund. Another 2% at 15 years on ₹15 lakh fund value = ₹30,000. Total loyalty additions = ₹46,000.

definition: An additional benefit attached to a base life insurance policy for extra coverage at an incremental premium. Common riders: accidental death, critical illness, waiver of premium, disability income, and hospital cash. IRDAI mandates that total rider premium cannot exceed 30% of the base policy premium.
example: Your ₹1 crore term plan costs ₹12,000/year. You add a critical illness rider (₹25 lakh cover) for ₹3,000/year and accidental death rider (₹50 lakh extra) for ₹1,500/year. Total premium: ₹16,500/year for comprehensive life + CI + accident coverage.

definition: A rider that waives all future premiums of the base policy if the policyholder becomes permanently disabled, is diagnosed with a critical illness, or (in some variants) dies. The policy continues with full benefits without any further premium payment. One of the most recommended riders for income earners.
example: You have a ₹1 crore endowment policy with ₹50,000 annual premium and waiver of premium rider. At age 40, you are diagnosed with cancer (a covered critical illness). The rider activates — all future premiums are waived. Your ₹1 crore policy continues to maturity with full bonuses, without paying another rupee.

definition: A rider that pays an additional sum assured on top of the base death benefit if the insured dies due to an accident. The accidental death must occur within a specified period (usually 90-180 days) of the accident. Does not pay for death due to illness, suicide, or natural causes.
example: Your ₹1 crore term plan has an accidental death benefit rider of ₹50 lakh. If you die in a car accident, your nominee receives ₹1.5 crore (₹1 crore base + ₹50 lakh accident rider). If you die of heart disease, the nominee receives only ₹1 crore (base benefit).

definition: A rider that pays a lump sum on diagnosis of specified critical illnesses such as cancer, heart attack, stroke, kidney failure, major organ transplant, and coronary artery bypass surgery. Payment is on diagnosis (not hospitalization). Typically covers 10-30 critical illnesses. The payout is a fixed amount, not related to actual medical expenses.
example: Your term plan has a critical illness rider of ₹25 lakh covering 20 illnesses. You are diagnosed with Stage 2 cancer. The insurer pays ₹25 lakh lump sum on diagnosis (regardless of actual treatment cost). This can be used for treatment, lost income, or any purpose.

definition: A rider that provides a monthly income benefit if the policyholder becomes totally and permanently disabled due to accident or illness. The monthly payout is typically 1% of the rider sum assured, paid for a specified period or until recovery. Helps replace lost income during disability.
example: Your life policy has a disability income rider with ₹50 lakh sum assured. You become permanently disabled in an accident. The rider pays ₹50,000/month (1% of ₹50 lakh) for up to 5 years = ₹30 lakh total. Your base life policy also continues with premiums waived.

definition: A rider available on term insurance plans that returns all premiums paid if the insured survives the policy term. Converts a pure protection term plan into a plan with a survival benefit. The rider significantly increases the premium (often 2-3x the base term premium) and reduces the effective IRR.
example: A 30-year term plan: Base premium ₹12,000/year. With return of premium rider, premium becomes ₹30,000/year. If you survive 30 years, you get back ₹9 lakh (30 × ₹30,000). But investing the ₹18,000/year difference in mutual funds at 12% would yield ₹52 lakh. ROP rider is rarely cost-effective.

definition: The minimum guaranteed amount the policyholder receives on surrendering a life insurance policy, as specified in the policy contract. Available after a minimum number of premiums are paid (usually 2-3 years). For traditional plans, GSV = 30% of total premiums paid (excluding first year's premium) × a factor that increases with policy duration.
example: A 20-year endowment, annual premium ₹40,000. After 5 years (₹2 lakh paid), GSV = 30% × (₹2L - ₹40,000 first year) = 30% × ₹1.6L = ₹48,000. This is the minimum you receive on surrender. Special Surrender Value is usually higher.

definition: A higher surrender value than the guaranteed amount, calculated by the insurer based on the policy's paid-up value, duration completed, prevailing interest rates, and accumulated bonuses. Usually significantly higher than GSV. IRDAI's 2024 regulations have improved SSV calculations to be more policyholder-friendly.
example: After 10 years of a 20-year endowment, GSV = ₹95,000 but SSV = ₹2.1 lakh. You receive the higher value — ₹2.1 lakh. Under IRDAI 2024 norms, SSV factors are more generous: a policy 50% through its term gets a higher proportion of the policy's intrinsic value.

definition: The market value of the units held in a ULIP policyholder's account, calculated as: Number of Units × NAV (Net Asset Value) per unit. Fund value fluctuates daily based on market performance. The policyholder bears the investment risk. Fund value determines the maturity benefit and partial withdrawal amounts.
example: You have 5,000 units in an equity fund with current NAV of ₹80. Fund value = 5,000 × ₹80 = ₹4 lakh. If the market rises and NAV becomes ₹95, fund value = 5,000 × ₹95 = ₹4.75 lakh. If NAV drops to ₹70, fund value = ₹3.5 lakh.

definition: The per-unit value of a ULIP fund, calculated daily as: (Total Fund Assets - Liabilities) / Number of Outstanding Units. Similar to mutual fund NAV. Different ULIP funds (equity, debt, balanced) have different NAVs. NAV determines the number of units allocated when premium is invested.
example: You pay ₹50,000 annual premium. After charges (₹5,000), ₹45,000 is invested. Equity fund NAV is ₹90. Units allocated = ₹45,000 / ₹90 = 500 units. Next year, if NAV rises to ₹110, these 500 units are worth ₹55,000 — a ₹10,000 gain.

definition: The process of converting a ULIP premium payment into investment units. After deducting premium allocation charges (if any) and other applicable charges, the remaining premium is divided by the prevailing NAV to determine units allocated. Higher NAV means fewer units; lower NAV means more units.
example: Premium: ₹1 lakh. Premium allocation charge: 3% (₹3,000). Amount invested: ₹97,000. NAV of chosen equity fund: ₹50. Units allocated: ₹97,000 / ₹50 = 1,940 units. These units grow or decline based on market performance.

definition: The cost of providing life insurance coverage (death benefit) in a ULIP or traditional policy. Deducted monthly from the ULIP fund value by cancelling units. Increases with age as the probability of death rises. Higher sum at risk (difference between death benefit and fund value) means higher mortality charge.
example: A 40-year-old with ₹50 lakh death benefit and ₹8 lakh fund value has a sum at risk of ₹42 lakh. Monthly mortality charge at age 40 might be ₹350/month (₹4,200/year). By age 55, it could be ₹1,500/month (₹18,000/year) as mortality risk increases.

definition: An annual charge deducted from the ULIP fund for managing the investment portfolio. Expressed as a percentage of fund value, charged daily by adjusting NAV. IRDAI caps FMC at 1.35% per annum for all ULIP funds. Lower FMC means more of the returns go to the policyholder.
example: Your ULIP fund value is ₹10 lakh. FMC is 1.35% p.a. Annual FMC = ₹13,500. This is deducted daily (₹37/day) by reducing the NAV. If the gross fund return is 12%, the net return after FMC is approximately 10.65%.

definition: A monthly charge deducted from the ULIP fund value to cover policy administration costs such as record-keeping, statement generation, and customer service. Deducted by cancelling units from the fund. Typically ₹200-500 per month, though some insurers charge a percentage of fund value.
example: Your ULIP deducts ₹400/month as policy administration charge by cancelling units. Annual impact: ₹4,800. On a fund value of ₹5 lakh, this represents nearly 1% additional charge on top of FMC, significantly eroding returns in the early years.

definition: An upfront charge deducted from each ULIP premium before investment. Covers distribution costs (agent commission), underwriting, and initial policy setup. IRDAI has significantly reduced this charge — most post-2010 ULIPs have 0-3% PAC vs 40-70% in older ULIPs. Only the remaining amount after PAC is invested.
example: Premium: ₹1 lakh. Premium Allocation Charge: 2% (₹2,000). Amount invested in funds: ₹98,000. In older ULIPs (pre-2010), PAC could be 60% in year 1 — only ₹40,000 of ₹1 lakh would be invested. IRDAI reforms made ULIPs much more transparent.

definition: A charge deducted from the ULIP fund value if the policy is surrendered before the lock-in period (5 years) or within a specified period. IRDAI mandates a 5-year lock-in for ULIPs — surrender before 5 years is not allowed (proceeds are paid after lock-in ends). Post lock-in surrender charges are nil for most ULIPs.
example: You want to exit your ULIP in year 3. Fund value: ₹4 lakh. Since the 5-year lock-in applies, you cannot get the money now. After the 5-year lock-in ends, the discontinued fund value (which earned minimal returns of 4%) is paid — approximately ₹4.5 lakh, less than if you had stayed invested.

definition: Withdrawal of a portion of the ULIP fund value without surrendering the policy. Allowed only after the 5-year lock-in period. Minimum sum at risk must be maintained. No limit on number of withdrawals, but minimum and maximum amounts may apply. Tax-free if the policy qualifies under Section 10(10D).
example: Your ULIP fund value is ₹12 lakh after 7 years. You withdraw ₹3 lakh for your child's college admission. Fund value reduces to ₹9 lakh. The policy continues with ₹9 lakh fund value. The ₹3 lakh withdrawal is tax-free as annual premium was ≤ ₹2.5 lakh and policy is older than 5 years.

definition: Changing the allocation of future premiums to different funds within a ULIP without affecting existing units. For example, redirecting future premiums from equity to debt fund as the policyholder ages. Usually free of charge. Different from fund switching which moves existing units.
example: Your ULIP premium of ₹1 lakh was allocated 80% equity + 20% debt. After 10 years (approaching maturity in 5 years), you redirect future premiums to 30% equity + 70% debt to protect gains. Existing units in equity remain unchanged — only new premiums go to debt.

definition: Moving existing units from one ULIP fund to another — e.g., from equity fund to debt fund or vice versa. Most ULIPs allow 4-12 free fund switches per year; additional switches may be charged ₹100-500 each. Useful for rebalancing portfolio based on market conditions or changing risk appetite.
example: Markets are at an all-time high and you want to book profits. You switch 50% of your equity fund units (₹6 lakh) to a debt fund. If markets then fall 20%, your equity portion loses ₹1.2 lakh but the ₹6 lakh in debt is safe. You can switch back when markets correct.

definition: A feature that keeps the ULIP's life cover active even when the fund value is insufficient to cover mortality charges, by reducing the sum assured proportionally. Prevents the policy from lapsing due to fund value depletion. The policy continues with a reduced death benefit.
example: Your ULIP fund value drops to ₹20,000 due to poor market performance and you have stopped paying premiums. The monthly mortality charge is ₹500. Instead of lapsing the policy, auto-cover kicks in — it reduces the sum assured from ₹25 lakh to ₹10 lakh, bringing the mortality charge down to ₹200/month, keeping the policy alive.

definition: An amount added to the policy's paid-up value when dividends or bonuses are used to purchase additional fully paid-up insurance within the policy. Common in participating whole life and endowment plans. Each paid-up addition increases the total death benefit and maturity value without additional premium outlay.
example: Your whole life policy's annual dividend of ₹12,000 is used to buy paid-up additions worth ₹15,000 of additional sum assured each year. After 20 years, paid-up additions total ₹3 lakh — increasing your effective death benefit from ₹10 lakh to ₹13 lakh at no extra cost.

definition: The maximum sum insured for a motor vehicle under an own damage insurance policy. Represents the current market value of the vehicle — calculated as the manufacturer's listed selling price minus depreciation based on vehicle age. IDV is the maximum the insurer will pay in case of total loss or theft.
example: A car purchased for ₹10 lakh in 2023. In 2026 (3 years old), depreciation is 30%. IDV = ₹10 lakh × (1 - 30%) = ₹7 lakh. If the car is stolen, you receive ₹7 lakh. If the car is damaged beyond repair (total loss), the insurer pays ₹7 lakh minus salvage.

definition: A cumulative discount on the own damage premium earned for each consecutive claim-free year in motor insurance. IRDAI-mandated NCB slabs: 20% (after 1 year), 25% (2 years), 35% (3 years), 45% (4 years), 50% (5+ years). NCB is transferable when switching insurers but resets to 0% if a claim is made.
example: After 5 claim-free years, you have 50% NCB. OD premium without NCB: ₹12,000. With 50% NCB: ₹6,000 — saving ₹6,000/year. If you make one small claim of ₹5,000, you lose the 50% NCB. Better to pay small claims out of pocket and protect the NCB.

definition: A motor insurance add-on that waives the depreciation deduction on replaced parts during a claim. Without this add-on, the insurer deducts depreciation (50% on rubber/plastic parts, 30% on fiberglass, etc.) from the claim amount. Also called 'bumper-to-bumper' or 'nil depreciation' cover. Adds 15-20% to premium.
example: Your 3-year-old car needs ₹50,000 in repairs after an accident — ₹20,000 for plastic bumper, ₹15,000 for rubber parts, ₹15,000 for body panels. Without zero dep, insurer deducts: ₹10,000 (50% on plastic) + ₹7,500 (50% on rubber) = ₹17,500 deduction. With zero dep, insurer pays full ₹50,000.

definition: A motor insurance add-on providing 24x7 emergency roadside services including towing, flat tyre change, battery jump-start, fuel delivery, key lockout assistance, and minor on-spot repairs. Available as an insurer add-on or standalone service. Typically covers 4-6 incidents per year with towing up to 50-100 km.
example: Your car breaks down on the Mumbai-Pune Expressway at midnight. You call the RSA helpline. Within 45 minutes, a technician arrives, diagnoses a dead battery, and provides a jump-start. If the car cannot be fixed on-spot, they tow it to the nearest authorized service center (up to 50 km) free of charge.

definition: A motor insurance add-on that covers damage to the vehicle's engine and gearbox due to water ingression (hydrostatic lock), oil leakage, or consequential damage. Standard motor policies exclude engine damage caused by water logging. Essential for vehicles in flood-prone cities like Mumbai, Chennai, and Kolkata.
example: During Mumbai monsoons, your car drives through a waterlogged road. Water enters the engine (hydrostatic lock), damaging pistons and crankshaft. Repair cost: ₹2.5 lakh. Without engine protect, this is excluded. With engine protect add-on (₹1,500-3,000/year premium), the full ₹2.5 lakh is covered.

definition: A motor insurance add-on covering the cost of consumable items used during vehicle repair — engine oil, coolant, brake oil, nuts and bolts, grease, clips, AC gas, and bearings. Standard motor policies exclude consumables, which can constitute 5-10% of a repair bill.
example: After an accident, car repairs total ₹1.2 lakh including ₹12,000 in consumables (engine oil ₹3,000, coolant ₹1,500, nuts/bolts ₹2,000, AC gas ₹2,500, brake oil ₹1,500, other ₹1,500). Without this add-on, you pay ₹12,000 out of pocket. With it, the full ₹1.2 lakh is covered.

definition: A motor insurance add-on that pays the difference between the IDV and the original invoice price (including registration and road tax) in case of total loss or theft. Without RTI, you receive only the depreciated IDV. Best suited for new vehicles (1-3 years old) where the IDV-to-invoice gap is significant.
example: Your car's invoice price was ₹12 lakh (ex-showroom ₹10 lakh + registration ₹1.5 lakh + road tax ₹50,000). After 2 years, IDV = ₹8 lakh. If stolen, without RTI: you get ₹8 lakh. With RTI add-on: you get ₹12 lakh (full invoice). The RTI add-on costs ₹2,000-4,000/year.

definition: A motor insurance add-on that covers the cost of replacing lost, stolen, or damaged vehicle keys and reprogramming the lock/immobilizer system. Modern car keys with transponders and smart keys can cost ₹10,000-50,000 to replace. Standard motor policies do not cover key replacement.
example: You lose the smart key of your SUV. Replacement cost from the authorized dealer: ₹35,000 (new key ₹20,000 + reprogramming ₹10,000 + lock replacement ₹5,000). With key replacement add-on (premium ₹500-1,500/year), the full ₹35,000 is covered.

definition: A motor insurance add-on covering damage to tyres from road hazards such as punctures, cuts, bursts, and sidewall damage. Standard motor policies exclude tyre damage unless it occurs along with other accidental damage. Covers tyre replacement or repair cost.
example: Your car hits a deep pothole, causing sidewall damage to two tyres. Replacement cost: ₹14,000 (₹7,000 per tyre). Without tyre cover, this is excluded from insurance. With tyre cover add-on (₹500-1,000/year), the ₹14,000 is covered after a small excess of ₹500.

definition: Coverage for windshield and window glass repair or replacement without affecting NCB. Some comprehensive policies cover the first windshield claim without depreciation. As a separate add-on, it may provide unlimited claims for glass damage. Windshield replacement costs ₹5,000-30,000 depending on the vehicle.
example: A stone chip cracks your car's windshield. Replacement cost: ₹18,000. Filing under OD cover would reset your 50% NCB, costing you ₹6,000+ on next renewal. With windshield cover add-on, the ₹18,000 is covered without affecting NCB — you save both the repair cost and NCB.

definition: Insurance covering passengers in the vehicle for accidental death and bodily injury. The compulsory PA cover (₹15 lakh) covers only the owner-driver. Passenger cover is an additional optional cover for named or unnamed passengers, providing a fixed sum assured per passenger per accident.
example: You have a 5-seater car with passenger cover of ₹2 lakh per passenger. In an accident, 3 passengers are injured. Each passenger is eligible for compensation up to ₹2 lakh for medical expenses or disability benefit. Without passenger cover, passengers have no coverage under your motor policy.

definition: Mandatory personal accident cover for the owner-driver of a motor vehicle, as required by the Motor Vehicles Act. Provides ₹15 lakh coverage for accidental death or permanent disability while driving or alighting from the vehicle. This cover is compulsory and cannot be opted out. Premium is fixed by IRDAI.
example: While driving, you are involved in an accident and suffer permanent total disability. The PA owner-driver cover pays ₹15 lakh as a lump sum. If you already have a standalone PA policy of ₹15 lakh+, you can opt for a reduced compulsory PA premium by providing proof of existing cover.

definition: The portion of motor insurance premium that covers liability to third parties — injuries or death to other people and damage to their property caused by your vehicle. TP premium rates are fixed by IRDAI and are mandatory for all vehicles. TP-only policy is the minimum legal requirement to drive in India.
example: For a private car (1000-1500cc), IRDAI-mandated TP premium is approximately ₹3,416/year (2025-26 rates). For a two-wheeler (75-150cc), TP premium is approximately ₹714/year. These rates are non-negotiable — every insurer charges the same TP premium as fixed by IRDAI.

definition: The portion of motor insurance premium that covers damage to your own vehicle from accidents, fire, theft, natural disasters, and other perils. Unlike TP premium (fixed by IRDAI), OD premium is market-driven — insurers compete on price. OD premium depends on IDV, vehicle age, NCB, city, and add-ons chosen.
example: For a ₹10 lakh car (2 years old, IDV ₹8 lakh), OD premium: ₹8,000 (base) minus 25% NCB (₹2,000) = ₹6,000. Adding zero dep (₹1,200) + engine protect (₹800) + RSA (₹500) = total OD premium ₹8,500. This is on top of the mandatory TP premium of ₹3,416.

definition: A fixed non-negotiable amount the policyholder must pay in every own damage claim, as mandated by IRDAI. For private cars ≤1500cc: ₹1,000; cars >1500cc: ₹2,000; two-wheelers: ₹100. Cannot be waived or covered by any add-on. Applied before the voluntary deductible.
example: You file an OD claim for ₹30,000 damage to your 1200cc car. Compulsory deductible: ₹1,000. Voluntary deductible (if opted): ₹5,000. Insurance pays: ₹30,000 - ₹1,000 - ₹5,000 = ₹24,000. The ₹1,000 compulsory deductible applies to every single claim.

definition: An optional additional deductible amount chosen by the policyholder to reduce the OD premium. Available options: ₹2,500, ₹5,000, ₹7,500, ₹15,000. Higher voluntary deductible = higher premium discount. Applied on top of the compulsory deductible for every OD claim.
example: Choosing ₹7,500 voluntary deductible gives a 15% OD premium discount. If base OD premium is ₹10,000, you save ₹1,500/year. But if you make 2 claims that year, you pay ₹7,500 × 2 = ₹15,000 extra in deductibles — ₹13,500 net loss. Recommended for careful drivers with low claim history.

definition: A motor insurance add-on that pays a daily cash allowance while your vehicle is being repaired at the garage after an insured claim. Compensates for the inconvenience and cost of alternative transportation (taxi, rental car) during the repair period. Typically ₹500-1,500 per day for 5-15 days.
example: Your car is at the service center for 10 days after accident damage. Garage cash add-on pays ₹1,000/day × 10 days = ₹10,000. You use this for Ola/Uber rides or car rental during the repair period. Without this add-on, you bear the transportation cost yourself.

definition: A fixed daily cash benefit paid to the policyholder for each day the insured vehicle is in the repair shop following a covered claim. Functionally identical to garage cash cover. Can be part of a comprehensive add-on bundle or a standalone benefit.
example: Your two-wheeler is being repaired for 7 days after an accident. Daily allowance: ₹300/day × 7 days = ₹2,100. This covers your auto-rickshaw fare to work while the bike is being fixed.

definition: A motor add-on providing support when your vehicle breaks down or is involved in an accident far from your home city. Covers hotel accommodation, travel expenses to return home, and vehicle transportation back to your city. Complements roadside assistance with additional financial coverage.
example: Your car breaks down 500 km from home during a family road trip. Outstation emergency cover provides: hotel stay for 2 nights (₹6,000), return journey by train/bus (₹4,000), and vehicle towing to your home city (₹15,000). Total: ₹25,000 covered.

definition: Additional coverage for aftermarket electrical accessories fitted to the vehicle that are not part of the manufacturer's standard equipment. Items like music systems, GPS trackers, LED lights, reverse cameras, and dash cameras need separate electrical accessories cover. Coverage is on depreciated value unless zero dep is added.
example: You install an aftermarket music system (₹25,000) and dash camera (₹8,000) in your car. You declare these to the insurer and pay extra premium for electrical accessories cover of ₹33,000. If the music system is damaged in an accident, the insurer reimburses after depreciation (or full if zero dep).

definition: Additional coverage for aftermarket non-electrical accessories such as alloy wheels, roof racks, seat covers, fog lamps (without electrical component), and body kits. Must be declared separately to the insurer with their value. Premium is charged as a percentage of the declared accessory value.
example: You install alloy wheels (₹40,000) and a roof rack (₹15,000) on your SUV — total non-electrical accessories: ₹55,000. You pay additional premium of ₹550 (approx 1% of ₹55,000). If the alloy wheels are damaged in an accident, the ₹40,000 replacement is covered under your motor policy.

definition: Additional coverage for an externally fitted CNG or LPG kit in a vehicle. The kit's value must be declared to the insurer and added to the IDV. Without this cover, damage to the CNG/LPG kit and any third-party liability arising from kit-related incidents is not covered.
example: You install a ₹60,000 CNG kit in your car. Without declaring it, the ₹60,000 kit is uninsured. By adding CNG kit cover, you pay approximately ₹600-900 additional premium. The kit is covered for damage, and any TP liability from gas leakage is also covered.

definition: Coverage for engine damage caused by water entering the engine's cylinders during floods or driving through waterlogged roads, causing the pistons to lock (hydrostatic lock). Standard motor policies exclude this. Engine protect add-on covers hydrostatic lock. Critical for vehicles in flood-prone cities.
example: Your car stalls in knee-deep water during Mumbai rains. You try to restart, causing water to enter the engine cylinders and seize the pistons. Engine replacement cost: ₹3.5 lakh. Standard policy: excluded. With engine protect/hydrostatic lock cover: fully covered after deductible.

definition: A situation where the vehicle is damaged beyond economical repair or is stolen and not recovered. The insurer declares a total loss when the repair cost exceeds 75% of the IDV. The insurer pays the full IDV and takes possession of the salvage (wrecked vehicle).
example: Your car with ₹6 lakh IDV is severely damaged in a flood. Repair estimate: ₹5.5 lakh (92% of IDV). The insurer declares total loss and pays ₹6 lakh. You hand over the damaged car to the insurer. The insurer recovers ₹80,000 by auctioning the salvage.

definition: A situation where the vehicle can technically be repaired, but the repair cost is so high (typically exceeding 65-75% of IDV) that it is not economically viable. The insurer treats it as a total loss, paying the IDV rather than the repair cost, and takes the salvage.
example: Your 4-year-old car (IDV ₹4 lakh) is in a major accident. Repair estimate: ₹3.2 lakh (80% of IDV). Rather than repairing, the insurer declares constructive total loss and pays ₹4 lakh minus salvage value. The damaged car is taken by the insurer for salvage.

definition: A motor insurance add-on that protects the accumulated No Claim Bonus from being reset to 0% after making a claim. Without NCB protector, even one small claim in a year erases years of NCB. Most NCB protectors allow 1-2 claims per year without NCB impact. Premium: ₹500-2,000/year.
example: You have 50% NCB after 5 claim-free years, saving ₹6,000/year on OD premium. Your car gets a minor scratch — repair cost ₹8,000. Without NCB protector: claiming ₹8,000 means losing 50% NCB = ₹6,000/year loss. With NCB protector (₹1,000/year): you claim ₹8,000 and keep your 50% NCB.

definition: A section of the Income Tax Act 1961 that provides tax deduction up to ₹1.5 lakh per financial year for specified investments and expenses, including life insurance premiums, ELSS mutual funds, PPF, EPF, NPS, NSC, home loan principal, and tuition fees. Available only under the old tax regime.
example: You pay ₹50,000/year premium for a ₹1 crore term plan, ₹60,000 to PPF, and ₹40,000 to ELSS. Total 80C eligible: ₹1.5 lakh. In the 30% tax bracket, tax saving = ₹1.5L × 30% = ₹45,000. The life insurance premium qualifies for 80C only if premium ≤ 10% of sum assured.

definition: A section of the Income Tax Act providing tax deduction for health insurance premiums. Limits: ₹25,000 for self + family (₹50,000 if any member is 60+). Additional ₹25,000-₹50,000 for parents' health insurance. Maximum deduction: ₹1 lakh if all insured are 60+. Available under old tax regime only.
example: You (age 35) pay health premium: ₹20,000 for family + ₹30,000 for parents (age 62). 80D deduction: ₹20,000 (self, max ₹25,000) + ₹30,000 (parents, max ₹50,000 as they are 60+) = ₹50,000. In 30% bracket, tax saving = ₹15,000. Preventive health checkup up to ₹5,000 also qualifies.

definition: A section of the Income Tax Act providing tax deduction for contributions to pension/annuity plans offered by life insurance companies. The deduction limit is subsumed within the overall ₹1.5 lakh limit of Section 80C. Only the invested portion of the premium (not mortality charges) qualifies.
example: You contribute ₹50,000/year to LIC's Jeevan Akshay annuity plan. This ₹50,000 qualifies under Section 80CCC. Combined with your other 80C investments (PPF ₹60,000 + ELSS ₹40,000), total 80C+80CCC = ₹1.5 lakh (capped). Tax saving at 30%: ₹45,000.

definition: A section of the Income Tax Act for tax deduction on contributions to the National Pension System (NPS) and Atal Pension Yojana. 80CCD(1): employee contribution up to 10% of salary (within ₹1.5L of 80C). 80CCD(1B): additional ₹50,000 deduction exclusive to NPS, over and above the ₹1.5L limit. 80CCD(2): employer contribution up to 14% of salary (no cap).
example: Salary: ₹12 lakh. You contribute ₹1 lakh to NPS. 80CCD(1): ₹1 lakh (within 80C limit of ₹1.5L). 80CCD(1B): additional ₹50,000 deduction (over and above ₹1.5L). Total extra tax saving from NPS: ₹50,000 × 30% = ₹15,000, making NPS the only investment with a ₹2 lakh total deduction.

definition: A section of the Income Tax Act that exempts life insurance maturity/death benefit proceeds from income tax, subject to conditions. For policies issued after 01/04/2012: annual premium must be ≤ 10% of sum assured. For policies issued after 01/04/2023: aggregate annual premium across all policies must be ≤ ₹5 lakh. Death benefit is always tax-free regardless of premium.
example: An endowment policy with ₹10 lakh SA and ₹80,000 annual premium (8% of SA — within 10% limit). Maturity proceeds of ₹18 lakh are fully tax-free under 10(10D). But a ULIP with ₹10 lakh SA and ₹3 lakh annual premium (30% of SA — exceeds 10%): maturity proceeds are taxable.

definition: The simplified tax regime introduced in Budget 2020 (updated in 2023) with lower tax rates but minimal deductions. Under the new regime, Section 80C, 80D, 80CCC, 80CCD(1B), HRA, and most other deductions are not available. Only standard deduction of ₹75,000, NPS employer contribution (80CCD(2)), and family pension deduction are allowed. Default regime from FY 2023-24.
example: Income ₹15 lakh. Old regime: tax ₹2.1L after 80C (₹1.5L) + 80D (₹50K) deductions. New regime: tax ₹1.5L with lower slab rates (0% up to ₹3L, 5% ₹3-7L, 10% ₹7-10L, 15% ₹10-12L, 20% ₹12-15L). If deductions < ₹3.75 lakh, new regime is better.

definition: The traditional income tax regime with higher slab rates but numerous deductions and exemptions including 80C (₹1.5L), 80D (health insurance), HRA, LTA, home loan interest (₹2L), 80CCD(1B) (₹50K for NPS), and more. Must be actively chosen — the new regime is now the default.
example: If your total deductions (80C: ₹1.5L + 80D: ₹50K + HRA: ₹2L + 80CCD(1B): ₹50K + home loan: ₹2L) = ₹6.5 lakh, the old regime saves more tax. But if deductions are only ₹2 lakh, the new regime's lower rates are more beneficial. Insurance premiums provide meaningful deductions only under the old regime.

definition: A flat deduction from gross salary income, available without any investment or expenditure proof. Under the new tax regime: ₹75,000 (from FY 2024-25). Under the old tax regime: ₹50,000. Replaces the earlier transport allowance and medical reimbursement. Available to all salaried employees and pensioners.
example: Your gross salary is ₹10 lakh. Under new regime, standard deduction of ₹75,000 reduces taxable income to ₹9.25 lakh. Under old regime, standard deduction is ₹50,000 — taxable income ₹9.5 lakh. The ₹75,000 standard deduction saves ₹7,500-22,500 in tax depending on your slab.

definition: Life insurance maturity proceeds that are exempt from income tax under Section 10(10D). Conditions: For policies after 01/04/2012, annual premium must not exceed 10% of sum assured. For policies after 01/04/2023, aggregate annual premium across all policies must not exceed ₹5 lakh. ULIPs with premium > ₹2.5 lakh/year are taxed as capital gains.
example: Your LIC endowment: SA ₹15 lakh, annual premium ₹1.2 lakh (8% of SA). Maturity payout ₹25 lakh is fully tax-free. But your friend's policy: SA ₹5 lakh, premium ₹1 lakh (20% of SA). Their maturity payout is TAXABLE as income, since premium exceeds 10% of SA.

definition: From FY 2021-22, ULIP policies with annual premium exceeding ₹2.5 lakh are treated as equity-oriented funds for tax purposes. Short-term capital gains (holding < 1 year) taxed at 20%. Long-term capital gains (holding > 1 year) exceeding ₹1.25 lakh are taxed at 12.5%. Applies only to non-exempt ULIPs.
example: Your ULIP annual premium is ₹5 lakh (exceeds ₹2.5L threshold). On maturity after 10 years, fund value is ₹15 lakh, total premium paid ₹50 lakh. Gain: ₹-35 lakh (loss, so no tax). But if fund value is ₹80 lakh, gain: ₹30 lakh. LTCG above ₹1.25 lakh: ₹28.75 lakh × 12.5% = ₹3.59 lakh tax.

definition: Tax on gains from sale of long-term capital assets. For equity/equity-oriented instruments (including non-exempt ULIPs): holding period > 1 year, gains above ₹1.25 lakh taxed at 12.5%. For debt instruments: holding period > 3 years. The LTCG framework affects insurance-linked investment decisions.
example: You surrender a high-premium ULIP after 8 years. Fund value: ₹20 lakh. Total premium: ₹12 lakh. LTCG = ₹8 lakh. Tax-free threshold: ₹1.25 lakh. Taxable LTCG: ₹6.75 lakh. Tax: ₹6.75L × 12.5% = ₹84,375. Effective tax rate on total gain: 10.5%.

definition: A method of adjusting the purchase price of an asset for inflation using the Cost Inflation Index (CII) published by the government, to reduce the taxable capital gains. Indexation benefit is available for debt instruments and real estate held long-term. Not applicable to equity-oriented instruments or ULIPs since Budget 2024.
example: You invested ₹5 lakh in a debt fund in 2019 (CII 289) and sold in 2025 (CII 363) for ₹8 lakh. Indexed cost = ₹5L × (363/289) = ₹6.28 lakh. Taxable gain = ₹8L - ₹6.28L = ₹1.72 lakh (vs ₹3 lakh without indexation). Tax saved: ₹25,600 at 20% rate.

definition: The maximum tax deduction limit under Section 80C of the Income Tax Act — ₹1.5 lakh per financial year. This ceiling applies to the combined total of all 80C-eligible investments: life insurance premium, PPF, ELSS, EPF, NSC, 80CCC pension contributions, home loan principal, tuition fees, and 5-year FD.
example: You pay ₹50,000 life insurance premium + ₹72,000 EPF + ₹40,000 PPF + ₹20,000 ELSS = ₹1.82 lakh total 80C investments. But the deduction is capped at ₹1.5 lakh. The remaining ₹32,000 provides no additional tax benefit. Plan investments to use exactly ₹1.5 lakh efficiently.

definition: The maximum tax deduction limits under Section 80D for health insurance premiums. Self + family: ₹25,000 (₹50,000 if any insured member is 60+). Parents: additional ₹25,000 (₹50,000 if parents are 60+). Maximum total: ₹1 lakh (if all insured are 60+). Includes preventive health checkup up to ₹5,000 within these limits.
example: You (35, paying ₹20,000 health premium) + Parents (both 65+, paying ₹45,000 health premium): Self deduction = ₹20,000 (within ₹25,000 limit) + Parents deduction = ₹45,000 (within ₹50,000 limit) = ₹65,000 total. Tax saving at 30%: ₹19,500.

definition: A tax deduction of up to ₹5,000 per financial year for preventive health checkup expenses under Section 80D. This is not an additional deduction — it is included within the 80D limits. Available for health checkups of self, spouse, children, and parents. Cash payment is accepted (unlike premiums which must be non-cash).
example: You pay ₹20,000 health insurance premium (non-cash) + ₹4,500 annual health checkup (cash payment). Total 80D claim: ₹20,000 + ₹4,500 = ₹24,500 (within ₹25,000 limit). The health checkup is the only 80D item where cash payment is accepted.

definition: Tax treatment of insurance premiums paid by a Hindu Undivided Family (HUF). Life insurance premiums paid by HUF for any of its members qualify for Section 80C deduction. Health insurance premiums paid by HUF for its members qualify under Section 80D. The HUF is treated as a separate taxpayer from its members.
example: Your HUF buys a ₹1 crore term plan for you (karta) at ₹15,000/year premium and health insurance at ₹25,000/year. The HUF claims ₹15,000 under 80C and ₹25,000 under 80D. You can separately claim your personal insurance premiums in your individual tax return.

definition: Tax treatment of keyman insurance — a life insurance policy taken by a company on the life of a key employee. Premiums paid by the company are a deductible business expense. However, if the policy is assigned to the keyman, the maturity proceeds become taxable income in the hands of the keyman. Death benefit received by the company is taxable as business income.
example: A company takes ₹5 crore keyman policy on the CEO at ₹2 lakh/year premium. Premium is a deductible business expense, saving ₹60,000 tax at 30%. If the CEO dies, the ₹5 crore benefit is received by the company as taxable income. If the policy is assigned to the CEO on retirement, maturity proceeds are taxable in the CEO's hands.

term: IRDAI (Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India)
definition: The statutory body established under the IRDAI Act 1999 that regulates and develops the insurance industry in India. Functions include: granting licenses to insurers and intermediaries, protecting policyholder interests, setting premium tariffs (for motor TP), approving insurance products, and ensuring insurer solvency. Headquartered in Hyderabad.
example: Before any insurer can sell a new health plan, IRDAI must approve the product (product filing with UIN). If an insurer rejects your valid claim, you can escalate to IRDAI through IGMS (Integrated Grievance Management System) at igms.irda.gov.in.

definition: IRDAI's online portal (igms.irda.gov.in) for policyholders to register complaints against insurers, intermediaries, and TPAs. If the insurer does not resolve your complaint within 15 days, you can escalate through IGMS. IRDAI monitors all complaints and can penalize insurers for poor grievance handling.
example: Your health claim was wrongly rejected by the insurer. You first complain to the insurer's grievance cell. After 15 days with no resolution, you file a complaint on IGMS with policy details, claim number, and supporting documents. IRDAI directs the insurer to re-examine the claim within 15 days.

definition: A quasi-judicial authority appointed by the government to resolve insurance disputes between policyholders and insurers. There are 17 ombudsman offices across India. The ombudsman can handle claims up to ₹50 lakh. The process is free, requires no lawyer, and decisions are binding on the insurer but not on the policyholder.
example: Your ₹8 lakh health claim was rejected citing non-disclosure. IGMS did not help. You approach the Insurance Ombudsman in your city with complaint, policy documents, and medical records. The ombudsman examines the case and orders the insurer to pay ₹6 lakh (partial acceptance). The insurer must comply.

definition: A time period after which the insurer cannot challenge or contest a health insurance claim based on non-disclosure or misrepresentation. For policies issued before 2020: 8 years. Under IRDAI's 2024 guidelines: the moratorium period has been discussed for reduction. After moratorium, only proven fraud can void a policy.
example: You bought health insurance in 2018 without disclosing mild asthma. In 2026 (8 years later), the moratorium has passed. Even if the insurer discovers the non-disclosure, they cannot reject any claim citing this — the moratorium protects you. Only deliberate fraud (forged documents) can override moratorium.

definition: The period during which the insurer can investigate and contest a claim on grounds of non-disclosure, misrepresentation, or fraud. Under Section 45 of the Insurance Act (as amended in 2024), the contestability period for life insurance is 3 years from policy inception or revival, whichever is later. Previously it was 5 years.
example: You buy a term plan without disclosing smoking history. If you die in year 2 (within contestability period), the insurer can investigate and reject the claim if they find evidence of smoking. If you die in year 4 (beyond 3-year contestability), the insurer must pay unless they prove outright fraud.

definition: The key provision in the Insurance Act 1938 governing the insurer's right to contest claims. After the contestability period (3 years for life, per 2024 amendment), no policy can be called into question on any ground whatsoever, except fraud. The burden of proving fraud lies with the insurer. This section protects long-standing policyholders from claim rejections.
example: A policyholder did not disclose hypertension when buying a policy in 2020. They die of heart attack in 2026 (6 years later). Under Section 45, the insurer cannot reject the claim based on non-disclosure since 3 years have passed. The nominee receives the full death benefit.

definition: A unique code assigned by IRDAI to every approved insurance product. Format: IIIXXXVVVVVVV/YYYY — where III = insurer code, XXX = product type, and YYYY = year. Every policy document must display the UIN. Used to verify that a product is genuinely approved by IRDAI and not a fraudulent scheme.
example: Star Health Comprehensive's UIN: SHAHLIP23026V012324. SHA = Star Health (insurer code), HLI = Health (product type), P23026 = product number, V01 = version, 2324 = year. You can verify any policy's UIN on IRDAI's website to ensure it is a legitimate product.

definition: A period after receiving the policy document during which the policyholder can review the terms and return the policy for a full refund if unsatisfied, with minimal deductions (stamp duty, medical exam costs, risk charges for the period of cover). Duration: 15 days for offline purchase, 30 days for online/distance purchase.
example: You buy a health policy online and receive documents on March 1. You have until March 30 (30 days for online purchase) to review and cancel. If cancelled on March 15, you receive a refund of the full premium minus ₹1,000 (pro-rata risk charge for 15 days + stamp duty).

definition: The right of a health insurance policyholder to switch from one insurer to another without losing the credit for waiting periods already served. IRDAI mandates that the new insurer must give credit for PED and specific disease waiting periods already completed. Application must be made 45-60 days before renewal date.
example: You have been with Insurer A for 3 years (PED waiting of 4 years served 3 years). You port to Insurer B. Insurer B gives you credit for 3 years of PED waiting — you only need to wait 1 more year for PED coverage. Your cumulative bonus may also be ported subject to the new insurer's terms.

definition: The legislation that established the Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority (IRDAI) as the statutory body to regulate the insurance sector in India. The Act opened the Indian insurance market to private players (previously monopolized by LIC and GIC), set solvency requirements, and defined the powers of IRDAI to protect policyholders.
example: Before the IRDA Act 1999, only LIC sold life insurance and GIC (with subsidiaries) sold general insurance. The Act allowed private companies (HDFC, ICICI, SBI) to enter insurance with up to 26% foreign investment (now increased to 74% via amendments). This competition reduced premiums and improved products.

definition: The primary legislation governing insurance business in India, covering registration of insurers, policy terms, policyholder protection, assignment and nomination, lapsation and revival, claims settlement, and penalties for violations. Key sections: 38 (assignment), 39 (nomination), 41 (rebating prohibition), 45 (contestability).
example: Section 39 of the Insurance Act 1938 governs nomination — it allows the policyholder to appoint a nominee who will receive the policy benefits on the policyholder's death. Section 45 limits the insurer's right to contest claims after 3 years. These protections exist because of this foundational Act.

definition: IRDAI regulations that define policyholder rights and insurer obligations. Key provisions: proposal form standards, free look period (15/30 days), policy document delivery within 30 days, claim settlement timelines (30 days from last document), grievance redressal within 15 days, interest payment on delayed claims (2% above bank rate).
example: Under these regulations, if an insurer takes 45 days to settle your claim (15 days beyond the 30-day mandate), they must pay interest at bank rate + 2% (approximately 8.5%) on the claim amount for the delayed 15 days. For a ₹5 lakh claim: interest = ₹5L × 8.5% × 15/365 = ₹1,747.

definition: The practice of selling an insurance product that is unsuitable for the customer, through misrepresentation of benefits, concealment of terms, or pressure tactics. IRDAI has strict penalties for mis-selling including agent license cancellation, insurer fines, and policy cancellation with full refund. Common mis-selling: selling endowment as 'high return investment' or ULIP without explaining charges.
example: An agent sells a 25-year endowment plan to a 28-year-old saying 'you will get 12% returns.' Actual IRR is 5%. The customer discovers this after 5 years and complains to IRDAI for mis-selling. IRDAI can order full premium refund with interest, cancel the agent's license, and fine the insurer.

definition: The unethical practice where an agent convinces a policyholder to surrender an existing policy and buy a new one, primarily to earn fresh commission. The policyholder loses surrender value, resets waiting periods, and may not qualify for the same coverage at a higher age. Prohibited by IRDAI.
example: An agent convinces you to surrender your 8-year-old health policy (₹10 lakh SI, all waiting periods completed) and buy a new policy with a different insurer. You restart 30-day initial waiting, 4-year PED waiting, and 2-year specific disease waiting. The agent earned ₹15,000 first-year commission. You lost 8 years of waiting period credit.

definition: The illegal practice of an insurance agent offering a portion of their commission back to the customer as an inducement to buy the policy. Prohibited under Section 41 of the Insurance Act 1938. Both the agent giving the rebate and the customer accepting it can be penalized.
example: An agent says: 'Buy this ₹50,000 premium policy through me, and I will give you ₹10,000 cashback from my commission.' This is rebating — illegal under Section 41. The agent's license can be cancelled, and the customer can face a fine of up to ₹500. Report such practices to IRDAI.

definition: A qualified actuary appointed by an insurance company and approved by IRDAI, responsible for valuing liabilities, certifying premium rates, ensuring solvency adequacy, and advising on product pricing and bonus declarations. Must be a Fellow of the Institute of Actuaries of India (FIAI). Mandatory for every insurer.
example: The appointed actuary of LIC determines that the bonus rate for Jeevan Anand should be ₹48/1000 SA for FY 2024-25, based on the fund's investment performance and mortality experience. This recommendation goes to LIC's board for final declaration.

definition: The process by which insurers submit new products or product modifications to IRDAI for approval. Under the 'Use and File' framework (2024), insurers can launch products and file documentation with IRDAI simultaneously, rather than waiting for prior approval. IRDAI can later withdraw the product if it finds issues.
example: An insurer designs a new cyber insurance product and launches it under 'Use and File' — the product goes live immediately while IRDAI reviews the filing. If IRDAI finds the terms unfair (e.g., excessive exclusions), it can order the insurer to modify or withdraw the product within 30 days.

definition: The process by which an insurer discontinues selling an insurance product, either voluntarily or by IRDAI directive. Existing policyholders must be given the option to continue or migrate to an equivalent product. The insurer cannot withdraw a product without providing 3 months' notice and an alternative.
example: An insurer withdraws a health plan due to heavy losses. 50,000 existing policyholders are notified 3 months in advance and offered migration to a new plan with similar benefits. If the new plan has higher premium, the insurer must justify the increase to IRDAI.

definition: Standardized insurance products mandated by IRDAI that every insurer must offer with identical features and uniform pricing. Examples: Arogya Sanjeevani (standard health plan, ₹1-5 lakh SI), Saral Jeevan Bima (standard term plan), Saral Pension (standard annuity). Ensures basic insurance access with transparent, comparable terms across all insurers.
example: Arogya Sanjeevani: every health insurer must offer this with identical coverage — ₹1-5 lakh SI, 30-day initial waiting, 48-month PED waiting, 5% copay for 60+, no room rent limit. You can compare only on premium, claims service, and network hospitals — product features are identical across all insurers.

definition: The annualized rate of return at which the net present value of all cash flows (premiums paid and benefits received) from an insurance policy equals zero. Used to evaluate the true return on investment-linked insurance products. A ₹10 lakh endowment plan with 5% IRR means your money grew at 5% per year — compare this to PPF (7.1%), FD (7%), or equity mutual funds (12%).
example: LIC Jeevan Labh: Annual premium ₹40,000, term 25 years, maturity ₹18 lakh. Total premium paid: ₹10 lakh. IRR = 4.8%. PPF at 7.1% for same ₹40,000/year for 25 years = ₹27.5 lakh. The endowment gave ₹18L vs PPF ₹27.5L — IRR reveals the true cost of mixing insurance with investment.

definition: A more accurate version of IRR that handles irregular cash flows with specific dates. Essential for evaluating insurance policies where premiums are paid at different intervals and benefits are received at varying dates. XIRR accounts for the exact timing of each cash flow, unlike IRR which assumes equal intervals.
example: A money-back policy: Premium ₹50,000/year for 20 years (paid on different dates each year). Survival benefits: ₹1.5L in year 5, ₹1.5L in year 10, ₹1.5L in year 15, and ₹4L + bonuses at maturity (year 20). XIRR = 5.2% — calculated using each cash flow's exact date. More accurate than simple IRR.

definition: The constant annual rate at which an investment grows from its beginning value to its ending value over a specified period. Used to compare insurance product returns with other investments. CAGR = (Ending Value / Beginning Value)^(1/Years) - 1. Does not account for multiple cash flows — use IRR or XIRR for policies with ongoing premiums.
example: A ULIP invested ₹5 lakh (lump sum) 10 years ago. Current fund value: ₹12 lakh. CAGR = (₹12L/₹5L)^(1/10) - 1 = 9.1%. Compare with Nifty 50 CAGR of 12% over the same period. The ULIP's charges reduced effective returns by 3% per year.

definition: The real return on an investment after accounting for inflation. Calculated as: Real Return = ((1 + Nominal Return) / (1 + Inflation Rate)) - 1. Crucial for evaluating long-term insurance policies where inflation erodes the purchasing power of future payouts. Indian average inflation: 5-6% per year.
example: Your endowment matures with 5% IRR. With 6% inflation, real return = ((1.05)/(1.06)) - 1 = -0.9%. Your ₹18 lakh maturity payout has LESS purchasing power than the ₹10 lakh premiums you paid over 25 years. You effectively lost money in real terms despite seeing a nominal gain.

definition: The return you forgo by choosing one investment over the best alternative. In insurance, the opportunity cost of buying an endowment (5% IRR) vs investing the same premium in mutual funds (12% CAGR) is 7% per year compounded. Understanding opportunity cost is critical for making informed insurance-vs-investment decisions.
example: Endowment premium: ₹50,000/year for 20 years. Endowment maturity: ₹18 lakh (5% IRR). If the same ₹50,000/year was invested in a Nifty 50 index fund at 12% CAGR: ₹40.3 lakh. Opportunity cost = ₹40.3L - ₹18L = ₹22.3 lakh. This is the real cost of the endowment.

definition: Calculating the point at which continuing a policy becomes more beneficial than surrendering it. Factors: surrender value received today vs remaining premiums to pay and future benefits expected. If the policy's future IRR (from today to maturity) exceeds the best alternative investment, it is better to continue.
example: Your endowment: 10 years remaining, annual premium ₹40,000, current surrender value ₹3.5 lakh, expected maturity ₹8.5 lakh. Remaining premiums: ₹4 lakh. Future IRR from today = ((₹8.5L)/(₹3.5L+₹4L))^(1/10) - 1 = 6.8%. If FD offers 7%, surrendering and investing in FD is marginally better.

definition: The current worth of a future sum of money, discounted at an appropriate rate (typically inflation or investment return rate). Used to evaluate the real value of future insurance payouts. Present Value = Future Value / (1 + discount rate)^years. Essential for comparing policies with different payout timings.
example: Your endowment will pay ₹20 lakh in 15 years. At 7% discount rate (PPF rate), present value = ₹20L / (1.07)^15 = ₹7.24 lakh. This means ₹20 lakh in 15 years is worth only ₹7.24 lakh in today's money. If total premiums exceed ₹7.24 lakh, the policy destroys value.

definition: The value of a current sum of money at a specified future date, given an assumed rate of return. Used to project how much today's premium payments will be worth at maturity or to estimate what coverage amount will be adequate after accounting for inflation.
example: You need ₹50 lakh health coverage today. With 6% medical inflation for 20 years, future value = ₹50L × (1.06)^20 = ₹1.6 crore. You need ₹1.6 crore health cover in 20 years to maintain the same purchasing power as ₹50 lakh today. Plan for annual SI increases.

definition: Interest calculated on the initial principal plus previously accumulated interest — 'interest on interest.' Understanding compounding is crucial for evaluating insurance returns vs alternative investments. Even a 2-3% difference in return rate creates massive differences over 20-30 year insurance policy terms.
example: ₹1 lakh invested at 5% (endowment IRR) for 25 years = ₹3.39 lakh. Same ₹1 lakh at 12% (equity mutual fund) for 25 years = ₹17.0 lakh. The 7% difference in rate leads to a 5x difference in final value over 25 years. This is the power of compounding — and the cost of low-return insurance products.

definition: Interest calculated only on the original principal amount, not on accumulated interest. Used in some insurance loan calculations and flat rate EMIs. Always compare with compound interest equivalent to understand the true cost or return. Simple interest rate is always lower than the equivalent compound rate.
example: A policy loan of ₹2 lakh at 9% simple interest for 3 years: Interest = ₹2L × 9% × 3 = ₹54,000. Total repayment: ₹2.54 lakh. The equivalent compound rate is approximately 8.3%. Some insurers quote simple interest on policy loans — always convert to compound for fair comparison.

definition: A financial product that provides regular periodic payments (pension) for a specified period or for life, in exchange for a lump sum investment (purchase price). Offered by life insurance companies. Types: immediate annuity (payments start immediately) and deferred annuity (payments start after accumulation phase). Key for retirement planning.
example: You invest ₹50 lakh in LIC's Jeevan Akshay immediate annuity at age 60. With joint life annuity option at 6.5% rate, you and your spouse receive ₹27,083/month (₹3.25 lakh/year) for life. If you die first, your spouse continues receiving the same pension until their death.

definition: A stream of cash flows that continues forever. In insurance context, used to understand the concept of 'income replacement' — the capital needed to generate a permanent income stream. Value of perpetuity = Annual Income / Rate of Return. Helps determine the ideal life insurance sum assured.
example: You need to replace ₹12 lakh annual income permanently for your family. At 6% safe return rate, required corpus = ₹12L / 6% = ₹2 crore. This means your term plan sum assured should be at least ₹2 crore to replace your income forever through investment returns.

definition: A method to calculate the ideal life insurance sum assured based on the present value of the insured's future income stream, adjusted for personal expenses, inflation, and investment returns. HLV = (Annual Income - Personal Expenses) × Present Value Factor for working years remaining.
example: Age 35, annual income ₹15 lakh, personal expenses 30% (₹4.5L), income contributing to family: ₹10.5L/year, expected to work 25 more years, discount rate 4% (income growth minus inflation). HLV = ₹10.5L × 15.62 (PV factor for 25 years at 4%) = ₹1.64 crore. Minimum term cover: ₹1.64 crore.

definition: A simplified method to calculate life insurance needs based on a multiple of annual income. Common rule: 10-15 times annual income. More conservative than HLV but easier to calculate. IRDAI recommends a minimum of 10 times annual income for term insurance.
example: Annual income: ₹12 lakh. Using 12x multiplier: recommended life cover = ₹1.44 crore. This ensures the family can invest the death benefit and generate an income stream close to your current contribution. At 6% return, ₹1.44 crore generates ₹8.64 lakh/year.

definition: A method to calculate life insurance needs based on the family's annual expenses, outstanding liabilities, and future goals — rather than the earner's income. Insurance need = Present Value of future expenses + Outstanding loans + Children's education/marriage fund - Existing assets.
example: Annual family expenses: ₹8L/year for 20 years, outstanding home loan: ₹30L, children's education: ₹25L, existing savings: ₹15L. Insurance need = PV of ₹8L×20 years (₹1.1 crore at 4% discount) + ₹30L + ₹25L - ₹15L = ₹1.5 crore. Buy a ₹1.5 crore term plan.

definition: A fixed monthly payment comprising both principal and interest, used to repay loans. Relevant to insurance when evaluating policy loans, premium financing (IPF), or when comparing insurance premiums to EMI obligations. Understanding EMI helps budget for insurance alongside other financial commitments.
example: Home loan: ₹50 lakh at 8.5% for 20 years. EMI = ₹43,391/month. You also need ₹15,000/month for insurance (term: ₹1,200 + health: ₹2,000 + motor: ₹800 + children: ₹1,000 = ₹5,000/month). Total EMI + insurance: ₹48,391/month. Insurance should not exceed 10-15% of net income.

definition: The process of spreading a loan or premium payment into equal installments over time. Each installment covers both interest and principal. In insurance, amortization applies to policy loans, premium financing arrangements, and structured settlement plans where claim payouts are spread over time.
example: You take a policy loan of ₹3 lakh at 9% interest, to be repaid in 36 monthly installments. Monthly payment = ₹9,540. In the first month: ₹2,250 goes to interest (9%/12 × ₹3L) and ₹7,290 to principal. Over time, the interest portion decreases and principal portion increases.

definition: An interest calculation method where interest is charged on the outstanding principal amount, which reduces with each payment. Provides the true cost of borrowing. Policy loans from insurers typically use reducing balance, while some premium financing uses flat rate. Reducing balance rate is always lower than the equivalent flat rate.
example: Policy loan: ₹2 lakh at 9% reducing balance. After paying ₹50,000 principal, interest is on ₹1.5 lakh (not original ₹2L). Total interest over 3 years: ₹28,500. Same loan at 9% flat rate: interest on original ₹2L for full term = ₹54,000 — nearly double. Always check if the rate is flat or reducing.

definition: An interest calculation method where interest is calculated on the original principal for the entire loan term, regardless of repayments made. Results in higher total interest than reducing balance method. A flat rate of 9% is equivalent to a reducing balance rate of approximately 16-17%. Beware of premium financing or policy loans quoted at flat rate.
example: Premium financing: ₹1 lakh at 9% flat rate for 2 years. Total interest = ₹1L × 9% × 2 = ₹18,000. Monthly EMI = ₹1.18L/24 = ₹4,917. The effective reducing balance rate is approximately 16.4% — much higher than the advertised 9%. Always ask for the effective rate.

definition: The actual annual interest rate accounting for compounding frequency. When interest compounds more frequently (monthly, quarterly), the effective rate is higher than the nominal rate. Essential for comparing different premium financing offers and policy loan terms quoted with different compounding frequencies.
example: A policy loan at 9% nominal rate compounded monthly: Effective rate = (1 + 0.09/12)^12 - 1 = 9.38%. At 9% compounded quarterly: Effective rate = (1 + 0.09/4)^4 - 1 = 9.31%. The more frequent the compounding, the higher the effective rate. Always compare effective rates.

definition: The return on an investment expressed as an annual percentage, accounting for the investment amount, payouts, and holding period. In insurance, yield is used to evaluate the return on ULIPs, endowment policies, and annuity products. Higher yield means better returns for the policyholder.
example: An annuity purchased for ₹30 lakh paying ₹2.1 lakh/year. Yield = ₹2.1L / ₹30L = 7%. Compare with FD yield of 7.5% — the annuity yields less BUT provides a guaranteed lifetime income, which an FD does not. Yield comparison must factor in the annuity's longevity guarantee.

definition: A quick mental math shortcut to estimate how many years it takes for money to double at a given interest rate. Formula: Years to double = 72 / Annual Return Rate. Useful for quickly evaluating insurance product returns and comparing with alternative investments.
example: Endowment policy IRR: 5%. Time to double: 72/5 = 14.4 years. Equity mutual fund CAGR: 12%. Time to double: 72/12 = 6 years. PPF at 7.1%: 72/7.1 = 10.1 years. Your endowment takes 14 years to double your money while equity does it in 6 years — this reveals why low-IRR insurance products are poor investments.

definition: The investment return after adjusting for inflation, representing the actual increase in purchasing power. Real Return ≈ Nominal Return - Inflation Rate. Critical for evaluating long-term insurance products. If an endowment gives 5% return and inflation is 6%, real return is -1% — you are losing purchasing power.
example: Your LIC endowment earned 5% nominal return over 25 years. Inflation averaged 6%. Real return = 5% - 6% = -1%. Your ₹18 lakh maturity has less purchasing power than the ₹10 lakh premiums you paid. A PPF at 7.1% nominal gives 1.1% real return — at least preserving purchasing power.

definition: The stated return on an investment before adjusting for inflation. Insurance agents often highlight nominal returns ('your ₹10 lakh becomes ₹25 lakh!'). Always convert to real return by subtracting inflation. A 5% nominal return with 6% inflation means a -1% real return — you are poorer in purchasing power terms.
example: Agent pitches: 'Invest ₹50,000/year, get ₹25 lakh after 25 years — 150% gain!' Sounds great, but the nominal return is only 5.1% (IRR). After 6% inflation, real return = -0.9%. The ₹25 lakh in 2050 has the purchasing power of only ₹5.8 lakh in today's terms. Always ask for the IRR, not the nominal amount.

definition: The quantity of goods and services that a unit of money can buy. Insurance payouts received in the future have reduced purchasing power due to inflation. A ₹1 crore sum assured today may be worth only ₹23 lakh in purchasing power terms after 25 years at 6% inflation. This is why sum assured should account for inflation.
example: You buy a ₹1 crore term plan at age 30 for 30-year term. If you die at age 60, the ₹1 crore your family receives has the purchasing power of only ₹17.4 lakh in today's terms (at 6% inflation for 30 years). Solution: buy an increasing sum assured option or periodically increase cover.

definition: The principle that a rupee today is worth more than a rupee in the future because today's rupee can be invested and earn returns. Fundamental to evaluating insurance products — ₹10 lakh received at maturity in 20 years is worth much less than ₹10 lakh today. All insurance comparisons should use present value or IRR.
example: Two policies offer ₹20 lakh maturity: Policy A in 15 years, Policy B in 25 years. At 7% discount rate: PV of Policy A = ₹7.24 lakh, PV of Policy B = ₹3.68 lakh. Policy A is nearly twice as valuable despite both paying the same ₹20 lakh — because you get the money 10 years earlier.

definition: The process of determining the present value of a future sum by applying a discount rate. Used in insurance to evaluate the true worth of future maturity benefits, survival benefits, and claim settlements in today's terms. Discount rate typically reflects the opportunity cost of capital or inflation rate.
example: Your endowment promises ₹15 lakh at maturity in 20 years. Discounting at 7% (PPF rate): Present Value = ₹15L / (1.07)^20 = ₹3.88 lakh. If total premiums paid = ₹6 lakh, the endowment destroys ₹2.12 lakh in value. The nominal 'gain' of ₹9 lakh is an illusion.

definition: A proprietary composite score developed by EAZR AI that evaluates the overall quality and suitability of an insurance product on a scale of 0-100. Combines multiple factors: insurer financial strength (solvency, CSR), product features (coverage breadth, exclusions), value for money (premium vs benefits), and customer experience (claim settlement speed).
example: Star Health Comprehensive: EAZR Score 82/100 — Insurer strength 85 (CSR 98.14%), features 78 (good coverage but some sub-limits), value 80 (competitive premium), experience 84 (strong network hospitals). Niva Bupa ReAssure 2.0: EAZR Score 87/100 — higher on features (no room rent limit) and value.

definition: A proprietary score by EAZR AI measuring the adequacy of a person's overall insurance coverage. Evaluates life cover vs income, health cover vs medical costs in their city, motor cover adequacy, and presence of critical illness and accident protection. Score range: 0-100. Below 50 indicates dangerous under-insurance.
example: A 35-year-old earning ₹20 lakh/year with only ₹25 lakh term plan and ₹5 lakh health cover: EAZR Protection Score = 28/100. Recommended minimum: ₹2 crore term + ₹20 lakh health + ₹50 lakh CI rider + ₹1 crore PA. After optimization: Protection Score rises to 85/100.

definition: EAZR AI's product that allows policyholders to pay insurance premiums through a financing arrangement rather than paying the full annual premium upfront. Spreads the premium into manageable monthly installments, enabling higher coverage. Particularly useful for large premiums on health or term insurance that might otherwise cause budget strain.
example: Your ideal health insurance costs ₹45,000/year — too much upfront. EAZR IPF lets you pay ₹4,125/month (including ₹4,500 financing cost). You get the full ₹20 lakh health cover from day 1 while paying in installments. No compromise on coverage due to budget constraints.

definition: EAZR AI's innovative product that provides a loan against the surrender value of a life insurance policy, allowing the policyholder to get immediate liquidity without actually surrendering the policy. The policy continues to earn bonuses and provide coverage while EAZR provides a loan equivalent to the surrender value.
example: Your LIC endowment has ₹3.5 lakh surrender value. Instead of surrendering (losing future bonuses of ₹6 lakh), EAZR SVF lends you ₹3 lakh against the policy. The policy stays active, continues earning bonuses, and you repay the SVF loan in installments. You get liquidity WITHOUT losing the policy.

definition: The difference between the insurance coverage a person needs and what they actually have. India's protection gap is one of the highest globally — the average Indian is insured for only 10-15% of their actual need. EAZR quantifies this gap and provides actionable recommendations to close it.
example: You need ₹2 crore life cover (based on HLV) but have only ₹25 lakh. Protection gap = ₹1.75 crore (87.5% under-insured). You need ₹25 lakh health cover (for metro hospital costs) but have only ₹5 lakh. Health protection gap = ₹20 lakh. EAZR recommends a ₹2 crore term plan (₹15,000/year) and ₹25 lakh health (₹12,000/year).
source: EAZR AI / Swiss Re Mortality Protection Gap

definition: A financial arrangement where a third party (like EAZR) pays the insurance premium on behalf of the policyholder, who repays in installments. Different from policy loan — premium financing involves a separate financier, while policy loan is from the insurer against the policy's own value. Enables higher coverage without upfront cash strain.
example: A ₹1 crore term plan costs ₹25,000/year, but paying in one shot is difficult. EAZR premium financing: you pay ₹2,300/month for 12 months (total ₹27,600 including ₹2,600 financing cost). The policy is active from day 1 with full ₹1 crore coverage. Cost of financing: 10.4% APR.

definition: Providing a loan to the policyholder equivalent to the surrender value of their life insurance policy, allowing them to access liquidity while keeping the policy active. Unlike surrendering (which terminates the policy), SVF preserves future bonuses, death benefit, and tax advantages. An EAZR innovation.
example: Your policy has ₹5 lakh surrender value and ₹12 lakh expected maturity (5 years away). Surrendering now: you get ₹5 lakh and lose ₹7 lakh future value. SVF: you borrow ₹4.5 lakh, keep the policy alive, receive ₹12 lakh at maturity, repay the loan (₹5.5 lakh with interest). Net gain: ₹6.5 lakh vs surrender.

definition: EAZR AI's process of analyzing a user's entire insurance portfolio and recommending changes to maximize coverage while minimizing cost. Includes: identifying redundant covers, filling protection gaps, replacing low-value policies with better alternatives, and restructuring premium payments through IPF/SVF.
example: Current portfolio: 3 endowment policies (₹1.5 lakh/year premium, ₹30 lakh cover, 5% IRR), 1 basic health plan (₹5 lakh SI). EAZR optimization: Keep 1 endowment (near maturity), surrender 2 (₹3L SV), buy ₹1 crore term (₹12K/year) + ₹20 lakh health (₹15K/year). Net: 3x more coverage, ₹1.2 lakh less annual premium.

definition: An EAZR AI metric that rates the overall quality of a user's insurance portfolio on a scale of 0-100. Factors: coverage adequacy across life, health, motor, and accident categories, cost efficiency (premium as % of income), product quality (EAZR Scores of individual policies), and portfolio balance (not over-concentrated in one type).
example: A user with ₹1 crore term + ₹15 lakh health + ₹50 lakh CI rider + motor + PA: Portfolio Health Score = 78/100. A user with 5 endowment plans and no term/health: Portfolio Health Score = 22/100 — all investment, no protection. EAZR provides a detailed improvement roadmap.

definition: An EAZR AI metric measuring whether the user's coverage amount is sufficient for their specific needs, considering income, location, family size, liabilities, and health profile. Separate scores for each category: Life, Health, Motor, Accident. A score of 80+ means adequately covered in that category.
example: You earn ₹15 lakh/year in Mumbai, have a ₹40 lakh home loan, and a family of 4. Life Coverage Adequacy: 35/100 (₹50L cover vs ₹2 crore needed). Health Coverage Adequacy: 45/100 (₹10L cover vs ₹25L needed for Mumbai hospitals). Motor: 90/100 (comprehensive with zero dep). Overall: 52/100.

definition: An EAZR AI metric evaluating the quality and reliability of an insurance company based on: claim settlement ratio (CSR), incurred claim ratio, solvency ratio, complaint ratio, network hospital count (health), premium growth rate, and years of operation. Helps users choose reliable insurers beyond just premium comparison.
example: Star Health: Insurer Quality Score 88/100 — CSR 98.14%, solvency 1.97x, 14,000+ network hospitals, 30 years in health. A new insurer with low CSR (85%), limited hospitals (3,000), but cheapest premium: Quality Score 55/100. EAZR recommends the higher quality insurer even if premium is 10% more.

definition: An EAZR AI metric measuring the comprehensiveness of an insurance product's features — coverage breadth, add-ons included, sub-limit freedom, restoration benefits, wellness programs, and modern treatment coverage. A higher score means the product offers more comprehensive protection with fewer exclusions and limitations.
example: Niva Bupa ReAssure 2.0: Feature Richness 92/100 — no room rent limit, unlimited restoration, 500+ day care, AYUSH full cover, worldwide emergency, wellness rewards. A basic Arogya Sanjeevani plan: Feature Richness 45/100 — standard features only, 5% copay for 60+, limited modern treatment.

definition: An EAZR AI metric that evaluates whether a policy's premium is justified relative to its coverage, features, and insurer quality. Calculated by comparing the policy's benefit-to-premium ratio against industry benchmarks. A high score means you are getting excellent coverage for the premium paid.
example: Care Supreme ₹10L SI at ₹12,000/year: Value Score 88/100 — excellent features at competitive premium. A comparable product from another insurer at ₹18,000/year with similar features: Value Score 62/100 — good product but overpriced. EAZR recommends Care Supreme for better value.

definition: An EAZR AI metric quantifying the financial risk a user faces from uninsured or under-insured events. Considers: uninsured medical expenses risk, income loss on death/disability, litigation exposure from motor accidents, and gaps in existing coverage. Higher score means higher unprotected financial risk.
example: A 40-year-old with no term plan (₹15L income), basic ₹3L health cover, and TP-only motor policy: Risk Exposure Score 92/100 (extremely high). After buying ₹1.5 crore term, ₹20L health, comprehensive motor: Risk Exposure drops to 18/100. EAZR prioritizes the highest risk gaps first.

definition: EAZR's proprietary native PDF extraction and analysis pipeline for insurance policy documents. ABSORB stands for: Analyze → Break down → Structure → Organize → Rate → Benchmark. Processes uploaded policy PDFs to extract key terms, coverage details, exclusions, and premium breakdowns without requiring any external API dependencies.
example: A user uploads their Star Health Comprehensive policy PDF. ABSORB pipeline: (1) Extracts text from PDF, (2) Classifies as health insurance, (3) Extracts sum insured (₹10L), waiting periods, exclusions, room rent terms, (4) Validates against KG data, (5) Scores the policy (EAZR Score 82), (6) Identifies gaps (no CI rider).

definition: The process of extracting structured data from unstructured insurance policy documents (PDFs, images) using the ABSORB pipeline. Extracted data includes: insurer name, product name, UIN, sum insured/assured, premium, policy term, waiting periods, exclusions, sub-limits, riders, nominee details, and special conditions.
