example: You upload your LIC Jeevan Labh policy PDF. Policy Extraction identifies: Product: Jeevan Labh (Table 936), SA: ₹10 lakh, Term: 25 years, Premium: ₹40,000/year, Riders: Accident Benefit ₹10L, Bonuses declared: ₹2.4L, Maturity date: 01/04/2045. All structured data is stored in EAZR's system for analysis.

definition: EAZR's Neo4j-based knowledge graph containing structured relationships between insurers, products, regulations, benchmarks, tax rules, and ombudsman offices. With 62 insurers, 1,207 products, 100 regulations, and 760 benchmarks, it enables the AI to make factual, source-traced recommendations rather than relying on LLM training data.
example: When you ask 'Compare Star Comprehensive and HDFC Optima Secure,' EAZR queries the Knowledge Graph for both products' exact features, premiums, CSR, network hospitals, and waiting periods — returning verified facts, not AI-generated approximations. Every data point traces to a source.

definition: EAZR's system of retrieving relevant insurance knowledge from a vector database (Qdrant with 847 chunks) before generating AI responses. Instead of relying solely on the LLM's training data, RAG fetches specific IRDAI circulars, policy wordings, tax rules, and claims processes to ground responses in verified, current information.
example: When you ask 'What is the moratorium period?', RAG retrieves the exact IRDAI circular text: 'IRDAI/HLT/CIR/090/03/2024 defines moratorium as...' and the AI uses this retrieved text to generate an accurate, source-cited response — not a potentially outdated answer from training data.

definition: EAZR AI's multi-agent system that generates personalized insurance recommendations based on user profile (age, income, family, city, health), existing coverage, risk analysis, and product benchmarks from the Knowledge Graph. Recommendations are ranked by EAZR Score and include specific premium quotes, feature comparisons, and coverage gap analysis.
example: Input: 32-year-old, ₹18L income, married, 1 child, Mumbai, no existing insurance. Recommendation: (1) ₹1.5 crore term plan — HDFC Click 2 Protect (₹11,000/year), (2) ₹15L family floater — Niva Bupa ReAssure (₹18,000/year), (3) ₹25L CI rider, (4) ₹15L PA. Total: ₹35,000/year = 2% of income.

definition: EAZR AI's guided claim filing support that helps policyholders through the entire claim process — from intimation to settlement. Includes: step-by-step document checklist, timeline tracking, insurer contact details, pre-filled claim forms, escalation guidance (IGMS, Ombudsman), and common rejection reason warnings.
example: You need to file a health claim. EAZR Claim Assistance: (1) Reminds you to intimate within 24 hours with insurer helpline number, (2) Provides document checklist: discharge summary, bills, prescriptions, KYC, (3) Tracks 30-day settlement deadline, (4) If delayed: auto-generates IGMS complaint draft, (5) Calculates interest due on delayed settlement.

definition: The ratio of an insurer's available assets to their required solvency margin (amount needed to meet all future obligations). IRDAI mandates a minimum solvency ratio of 1.5x (150%). A higher ratio indicates greater financial stability. Solvency ratio is published quarterly by all insurers and monitored by IRDAI.
example: LIC's solvency ratio: 1.87x (above 1.5x minimum — financially stable). A new insurer with solvency 1.52x (barely above minimum) is more risky. If solvency falls below 1.5x, IRDAI restricts the insurer from writing new business until it is rectified. Always check solvency before buying a policy.

definition: The percentage of claims settled by an insurer out of total claims received in a financial year. Published annually by IRDAI. For life insurance, CSR measures death claims paid vs received. Higher CSR means the insurer is more likely to pay your claim. LIC CSR: 98.74%. Best private insurer CSR: 99%+.
example: Insurer A: CSR 98% — out of 1,000 death claims received, 980 were paid, 20 rejected. Insurer B: CSR 88% — out of 1,000 claims, only 880 paid, 120 rejected. You have a 12% chance of claim rejection with Insurer B. Always prefer CSR above 95% for life and 90% for health.

definition: The ratio of claims incurred (paid + outstanding) to premiums earned by a health insurer. Indicates what percentage of premium collected goes toward paying claims. Ideal range: 50-80%. Below 50%: insurer is collecting too much premium relative to claims. Above 100%: insurer is losing money on claims (unsustainable).
example: Star Health ICR: 68% — for every ₹100 of premium collected, ₹68 goes toward claims. This is healthy. An insurer with 45% ICR is charging too much premium for the coverage. An insurer with 110% ICR is paying more in claims than it collects — may increase premiums sharply next year.

definition: A hospital that has a tie-up agreement with an insurer or TPA to provide cashless claim facility. Treatment at network hospitals allows direct billing to the insurer. Non-network hospitals require reimbursement. Larger network = more hospital choices. Major Indian networks: Medi Assist (8,000+), Vidal Health (6,000+).
example: Star Health has 14,000+ network hospitals. If you are admitted to one of these, you show your health card and the hospital bills Star Health directly. At a non-network hospital, you pay ₹3 lakh upfront and submit bills for reimbursement — a cash flow burden. Always check network hospitals in your city before buying.

definition: Items and expenses in a hospital bill that the health insurer will not reimburse, regardless of the sum insured. IRDAI has published a standardized list of non-payable items including toiletries, personal comfort items (TV, phone), gown charges, documentation fees, and some administrative charges. Also called 'non-medical items.'
example: Your hospital bill includes: treatment ₹2 lakh + room charges ₹30,000 + toiletries kit ₹500 + gown ₹300 + TV charges ₹200 + documentation ₹1,000. Non-payable items: ₹2,000 (toiletries + gown + TV + documentation). Insurer pays ₹2.3 lakh minus ₹2,000 = ₹2.28 lakh.

definition: A single health insurance policy covering the entire family under one shared sum insured. Members typically include: self, spouse, and dependent children. Some policies allow parents. The sum insured is shared — any member can use it. More cost-effective than individual policies but carries the risk of SI exhaustion if multiple members claim.
example: Family floater: ₹15 lakh SI covering you, spouse, and 2 children at ₹18,000/year. If you claim ₹8 lakh, remaining SI for the entire family = ₹7 lakh. Individual policies for all 4 members at ₹15L each would cost ₹50,000+ but provide ₹60 lakh total coverage. Floater is cheaper but riskier.

definition: A health insurance plan with a per-claim deductible that provides additional coverage above the deductible for each individual hospitalization. Different from super top-up: top-up deductible applies to EACH claim independently, while super top-up uses aggregate deductible across all claims in the year.
example: Top-up: ₹20 lakh SI, ₹5 lakh per-claim deductible. Claim 1: ₹8 lakh → insurer pays ₹3 lakh. Claim 2: ₹4 lakh → insurer pays ₹0 (below ₹5L deductible). Claim 3: ₹7 lakh → insurer pays ₹2 lakh. Each claim's deductible is independent. Super top-up would aggregate all three claims.

definition: Health insurance provided by an employer to employees (and sometimes their families) as part of the employment benefits package. Features: no pre-existing disease waiting period, no medical underwriting, premium paid by employer. Coverage ends when employment ends. Should be supplemented with personal health insurance for continuous coverage.
example: Your employer provides ₹5 lakh group health cover for you and family. No PED waiting, no medical tests. But if you change jobs, coverage ends immediately. Recommended: maintain a personal ₹10 lakh policy alongside group cover. When you leave, the personal policy provides uninterrupted coverage.

definition: A policy that pays a lump sum on diagnosis of specified critical illnesses like cancer, heart attack, stroke, kidney failure, and major organ transplant. Payment is on DIAGNOSIS, not on hospitalization. The lump sum can be used for treatment, income replacement, or any purpose. Available as standalone policy or rider to life/health plans.
example: You have a standalone critical illness policy covering 30 diseases with ₹25 lakh sum insured. You are diagnosed with early-stage cancer. The insurer pays ₹25 lakh lump sum on receiving the diagnosis report — regardless of actual treatment cost. You use ₹15 lakh for treatment and ₹10 lakh for living expenses during recovery.

definition: Insurance providing financial protection against accidental death, permanent total disability, permanent partial disability, and temporary total disability. Pays a fixed sum insured (not related to medical expenses). Different from health insurance which covers treatment costs. PA insurance pays even if there are no medical expenses (e.g., immediate death).
example: You have a PA policy with ₹50 lakh cover. You lose one hand in an accident (permanent partial disability at 50% scale). PA payout: ₹25 lakh (50% of ₹50L). Your health insurance separately covers the ₹5 lakh hospital bill. PA and health are complementary — you can claim from both for the same accident.

definition: A government-sponsored personal accident insurance scheme available to all Indian bank account holders aged 18-70. Premium: ₹20/year (auto-debited from bank account). Coverage: ₹2 lakh for accidental death, ₹2 lakh for permanent total disability, ₹1 lakh for permanent partial disability. The cheapest PA cover in India.
example: You enroll for PMSBY through your bank's net banking for ₹20/year. You die in a road accident. Your nominee receives ₹2 lakh from PMSBY. This is in addition to any other insurance you have. For ₹20/year, every Indian with a bank account should have this basic PA cover.

term: PMJJBY (Pradhan Mantri Jeevan Jyoti Bima Yojana)
definition: A government-sponsored life insurance scheme providing ₹2 lakh life cover for death due to any cause. Available to bank account holders aged 18-50. Premium: ₹436/year (auto-debited). Unlike PMSBY (accident only), PMJJBY covers death from illness, disease, or accident. Renewable annually until age 55.
example: You and your spouse both enroll for PMJJBY at ₹436/year each. Total: ₹872/year for ₹4 lakh family life cover. While this is not sufficient (you need 10-15x income), it provides basic cover for those who cannot afford traditional term plans. PMJJBY + PMSBY = ₹456/year for ₹4L life + ₹2L PA cover.

definition: Pure life insurance that pays the sum assured ONLY if the insured dies during the policy term. No maturity benefit, no survival benefit, no investment component — 100% protection. Cheapest form of life insurance: ₹1 crore cover for a 30-year-old costs only ₹8,000-15,000/year. IRDAI and financial planners unanimously recommend term insurance as the foundation of life cover.
example: A 30-year-old male buys a ₹1 crore term plan for 30 years at ₹10,000/year. If he dies at age 45, his family receives ₹1 crore. If he survives to 60, the policy expires and premiums are not returned. Compare: an endowment providing the same ₹1 crore death benefit costs ₹3-4 lakh/year. Term gives 30x more cover per rupee.

definition: A life insurance policy combining protection (death benefit) with savings (maturity benefit). Pays sum assured + bonuses on death or on surviving the policy term. Returns are typically low (IRR 4-6%) compared to pure investments (12% equity, 7% PPF). Premium is 20-30x higher than a term plan for the same death benefit.
example: LIC Jeevan Labh: SA ₹10 lakh, 25-year term, premium ₹40,000/year. If you die: family gets ₹10L + bonuses. If you survive: maturity ₹18 lakh (IRR ~5%). The same ₹40,000/year in term plan (₹10,000) + mutual fund (₹30,000) gives ₹10L death cover + ₹28L maturity at 12% CAGR.

definition: A life insurance product that combines protection with market-linked investment. Part of the premium provides life cover; the remainder is invested in equity, debt, or balanced funds chosen by the policyholder. Returns depend on market performance. 5-year lock-in period mandated by IRDAI. Max FMC: 1.35%. Policyholder bears investment risk.
example: HDFC Click 2 Invest ULIP: ₹1 lakh/year premium. ₹10 lakh life cover. After charges (3%), ₹97,000 invested in equity fund. After 15 years at 10% net return: fund value ₹31 lakh. After charges, effective return ≈ 8-9%. Compare with direct equity mutual fund: 12% = ₹40 lakh. ULIP underperforms by ₹9 lakh.

definition: A life insurance policy that pays survival benefits at regular intervals (every 4-5 years) during the policy term, with the remaining sum assured and bonuses paid at maturity. If death occurs during the term, the full sum assured is paid regardless of survival benefits already received. IRR typically 4-5%.
example: LIC Money Back 20-year: SA ₹5 lakh. Survival benefits: 20% of SA (₹1L) at years 5, 10, 15. Maturity: 40% of SA (₹2L) + bonuses at year 20. Total premiums: ₹3.5 lakh. Total returns: ₹5L + bonuses ≈ ₹7.5L. IRR: ~5%. Liquidity every 5 years is the main attraction.

definition: A life insurance policy that provides coverage for the entire lifetime of the insured (typically up to age 99-100), unlike term plans which cover a fixed period. Builds cash value over time. Premium is higher than term but provides permanent coverage. Some whole life policies allow premium payment for a limited period (e.g., 15 years) with lifetime coverage.
example: LIC Jeevan Umang: Whole life policy, premium paying term 15-25 years. After premium payment, you receive 8% of SA as annual survival benefit every year for life. On death (at any age up to 100), nominee receives full SA + bonuses. Provides lifetime income + permanent death cover — but at a high premium.

definition: A life insurance policy that participates in the insurer's profits through bonus declarations. The policyholder receives reversionary bonuses declared from the insurer's surplus each year. Most traditional LIC policies (Jeevan Anand, Jeevan Labh) are participating policies. Non-participating policies have guaranteed returns but no bonus upside.
example: An LIC participating policy with ₹10 lakh SA receives annual bonus of ₹48/1000 = ₹48,000/year. Over 20 years, bonuses total ₹9.6 lakh — nearly matching the SA itself. A non-participating policy would have only guaranteed additions, which are typically lower. The trade-off: bonus rates are not guaranteed and can vary yearly.

definition: A life insurance policy that does not participate in the insurer's profits and therefore does not receive any bonus. Benefits are fixed and guaranteed at the time of purchase. Provides certainty of returns but typically offers lower total payout than participating policies in good years. Term plans are inherently non-participating.
example: A non-participating endowment with ₹10 lakh SA and guaranteed additions of ₹40/1000/year for 20 years: guaranteed maturity = ₹10L + ₹8L (additions) = ₹18L. No bonus uncertainty. Compare with participating policy: same SA but maturity ranges from ₹16-20L depending on bonus declarations.

definition: In a ULIP, the difference between the death benefit (sum assured) and the current fund value. Mortality charges are calculated on the sum at risk, not the full sum assured. As fund value grows, sum at risk decreases, reducing mortality charges. When fund value exceeds sum assured, mortality charge becomes zero.
example: SA: ₹25 lakh. Fund value: ₹8 lakh. Sum at risk = ₹25L - ₹8L = ₹17 lakh. Mortality charge is calculated on ₹17 lakh. If fund value grows to ₹20 lakh, sum at risk = ₹5 lakh — mortality charge drops significantly. If fund value exceeds ₹25 lakh, death benefit = 105% of fund value, no additional mortality charge.

definition: The mandatory 5-year period during which a ULIP policyholder cannot surrender or withdraw from the policy. Mandated by IRDAI since 2010 to prevent ULIPs from being used as short-term speculative instruments. If the policyholder stops paying premiums during lock-in, the fund is moved to a 'discontinued fund' with minimal returns (4%) and released after 5 years.
example: You invest ₹1 lakh/year in a ULIP starting 2024. You cannot withdraw any amount until 2029 (5-year lock-in). If you stop paying in year 2, your fund value moves to a discontinued fund earning only 4% until 2029, when it is paid out. You lose the opportunity cost of market returns.

definition: A segregated fund in ULIPs where the fund value is parked when a policyholder stops paying premiums during the 5-year lock-in period. The discontinued fund earns a minimum guaranteed return (currently 4% p.a., set by IRDAI). The fund value is released to the policyholder only after the 5-year lock-in ends.
example: You paid 2 years of ULIP premium (₹2 lakh), then stopped. Fund value at discontinuation: ₹1.8 lakh. This moves to the discontinued fund, earning 4%/year for 3 remaining years. After 5-year lock-in, you receive ₹2.02 lakh. If you had stayed invested in equity fund at 12%, it would be ₹2.53 lakh. Discontinued fund penalty: ₹51,000.

definition: The option to convert a portion of the pension corpus into a lump sum at the time of vesting (retirement). In NPS: up to 60% can be commuted (withdrawn as lump sum, tax-free). The remaining 40% must be used to purchase an annuity. In traditional pension plans: commutation is typically 33% of the corpus.
example: Your NPS corpus at retirement (age 60): ₹80 lakh. You commute 60%: ₹48 lakh (tax-free lump sum). Remaining 40%: ₹32 lakh used to buy an annuity at 6.5% = ₹17,333/month pension for life. The lump sum can be invested for growth; the annuity provides guaranteed monthly income.

definition: The percentage rate at which a lump sum (purchase price) is converted into periodic pension payments. Higher annuity rate means higher pension per lakh invested. Current annuity rates in India: 5.5-7.5% depending on age, type (single/joint life), and insurer. Rates have been declining with interest rates.
example: You invest ₹50 lakh in an immediate annuity at 6.5% rate. Annual pension = ₹50L × 6.5% = ₹3.25 lakh (₹27,083/month). At 7.5% rate: ₹3.75 lakh/year (₹31,250/month). The 1% rate difference = ₹50,000/year for life. Always compare annuity rates across LIC, SBI Life, HDFC Life, and ICICI Pru.

definition: A government-backed retirement savings scheme regulated by PFRDA, open to all Indian citizens aged 18-70. Contributions are invested in equity, corporate bonds, and government securities. Tax benefits: 80CCD(1) within 80C limit + 80CCD(1B) additional ₹50,000. At retirement, 60% can be withdrawn tax-free; 40% must buy annuity.
example: You start NPS at age 30, contributing ₹10,000/month in aggressive allocation (75% equity, 25% debt). At age 60: estimated corpus ₹1.5 crore (at 10% return). Commute 60%: ₹90 lakh (tax-free). Annuitize 40%: ₹60 lakh at 6.5% = ₹32,500/month pension for life. Tax saved during accumulation: ₹5.4 lakh over 30 years.

definition: Insurance covering risks associated with domestic or international travel including medical emergencies abroad, trip cancellation/interruption, baggage loss/delay, passport loss, personal liability, and adventure sports. Mandatory for Schengen visa (€30,000 minimum medical coverage). Available as single-trip or multi-trip annual policies.
example: You travel to Europe for 15 days. Travel insurance: ₹2,000 for $100,000 medical cover + $2,000 trip cancellation + $1,500 baggage loss. You fall sick in Paris — ₹5 lakh hospital bill covered by the insurer. Without travel insurance, you would pay ₹5 lakh out of pocket in a foreign country.

definition: Mandatory travel insurance for visiting Schengen area countries (26 European nations). Requirements: minimum €30,000 (approximately ₹27 lakh) medical coverage, valid for the entire stay plus 15 days, covering medical repatriation and emergency evacuation. Must be from an IRDAI-registered or EU-recognized insurer.
example: You apply for a French tourist visa. Schengen insurance requirement: €30,000 medical cover for 20-day trip. Policy cost: ₹1,500-3,000. Coverage: medical expenses €30,000, repatriation €50,000, personal liability €50,000. Without valid Schengen insurance, the visa will be rejected.

definition: Transportation of a patient back to their home country for continued treatment, or transportation of mortal remains in case of death abroad. Covered under travel insurance. Repatriation costs can be enormous — ₹10-50 lakh depending on distance and medical condition. One of the most critical travel insurance benefits.
example: An Indian tourist suffers a severe stroke in the US. After initial stabilization ($50,000), medical repatriation to India costs $25,000 (air ambulance with medical team). Total: $75,000 (₹63 lakh). Travel insurance with medical repatriation covers the entire cost. Without it: financial catastrophe for the family.

definition: Reimbursement of non-refundable travel expenses (flights, hotels, tours) if the trip is cancelled due to covered reasons: serious illness, death of family member, natural disaster at destination, visa rejection (some policies), or job loss. Covers actual financial loss up to the policy limit.
example: You booked a family trip to Thailand: flights ₹1.2 lakh + hotel ₹60,000 + tours ₹20,000 = ₹2 lakh. Three days before departure, your parent is hospitalized. Trip cancellation cover reimburses the non-refundable ₹1.8 lakh (₹20,000 of tours was refundable). Without cover: ₹1.8 lakh lost.

definition: Compensation for permanent loss, theft, or damage to checked-in or carry-on baggage during travel. Covers the cost of replacing lost items up to the policy limit (typically $500-$3,000). Delayed baggage cover provides a per-day allowance for purchasing essentials until baggage is recovered.
example: Your checked luggage is lost by the airline during a London trip. Contents: clothes, electronics, and personal items worth ₹80,000. Travel insurance baggage loss cover pays ₹60,000 (after ₹20,000 depreciation on electronics). The airline's liability is limited to $20/kg. Insurance fills the gap.

definition: The standardized percentage scale used by insurers to determine the payout for different types of permanent disabilities under personal accident insurance. Permanent total disability (loss of both limbs/eyes): 100%. Loss of one limb: 50%. Loss of sight in one eye: 50%. Loss of hearing in both ears: 50%. Loss of one thumb: 25%.
example: You have a ₹50 lakh PA policy and lose one arm in an accident. The disability scale assigns 50% for loss of one limb. PA payout: ₹50L × 50% = ₹25 lakh. If you also lost sight in one eye (50%) in the same accident: combined disability = 100% (capped). Total payout: ₹50 lakh.

definition: A condition where the insured is permanently and completely unable to engage in any gainful employment due to accident. Under PA insurance, this triggers 100% of the sum insured. Common qualifying conditions: loss of both hands, both feet, both eyes, or any combination of two. Spinal cord injury causing complete paralysis.
example: A construction worker falls from height and suffers spinal cord injury causing permanent paraplegia. His ₹20 lakh PA policy pays 100% = ₹20 lakh as lump sum. Additionally, if he has a disability income rider, he receives ₹20,000/month for 5 years. The PA payout is independent of health insurance claims for the same accident.

definition: A risk classification system in PA insurance that groups occupations by accident risk level. Class 1 (lowest risk): office workers, teachers, doctors. Class 2 (moderate): salespersons, supervisors, light manual work. Class 3 (high risk): factory workers, drivers, construction. Higher occupation class = higher premium. Some occupations are excluded.
example: An IT professional (Class 1) pays ₹3,000/year for ₹50 lakh PA cover. A delivery rider (Class 2) pays ₹5,500 for the same cover. A construction worker (Class 3) pays ₹8,000 and may have restricted terms. A stunt performer may be excluded entirely. Always disclose your exact occupation accurately.

definition: IRDAI's standardized list of 16 conditions and treatments that are permanently excluded from health insurance coverage, regardless of waiting periods. Includes: cosmetic surgery, war/nuclear risks, self-inflicted injuries, substance abuse treatment (voluntary), sex change surgery, obesity treatment (unless life-threatening), and congenital conditions (external).
example: You want rhinoplasty (nose job) for cosmetic purposes — permanently excluded under Exclusion List I. However, if the same surgery is reconstructive after a fracture/accident, it IS covered. The key distinction: cosmetic (excluded) vs medically necessary (covered). Always get a doctor's letter establishing medical necessity.

definition: IRDAI's standardized list of 17 conditions with specific waiting periods (typically 24 months from policy start). Includes: cataract, benign ENT disorders, sinusitis, joint replacement, kidney stones, gallstones, hernia, piles, fistula, hydrocele, uterine conditions, and congenital internal diseases. Covered after the specified waiting period.
example: Your policy has a 2-year specific disease waiting period for cataract (Exclusion List II). You buy the policy on 01/01/2024. Cataract surgery on 15/06/2025 (before 2 years) — rejected. Cataract surgery on 15/01/2026 (after 2 years) — covered. Portability credit from previous insurer reduces this waiting period.

definition: A waiting period (typically 24 months) that applies to specific diseases and conditions listed in Exclusion List II. Different from the initial waiting period (30 days for all illnesses) and PED waiting period (24-48 months for pre-existing conditions). Claims for listed diseases within this period are rejected.
example: Your policy lists hernia under 2-year specific disease waiting. You develop a hernia 18 months after buying the policy. The claim is rejected — 6 months of waiting remain. If you port to a new insurer, you carry 18 months of credit, needing only 6 more months. After 24 months total, hernia is fully covered.

definition: Under the Insurance Laws (Amendment) Act 2015, certain nominees are classified as 'beneficial nominees' — they become the absolute beneficiaries of the policy proceeds, not just recipients. Beneficial nominees include: spouse, children, and parents. Other nominees (like siblings, friends) are simply trustees for legal heirs.
example: You nominate your wife as nominee. As a spouse, she is a beneficial nominee — the ₹1 crore death benefit is hers absolutely. No other family member (like your siblings) can claim it. If instead you had nominated a friend, they would only be a trustee — legal heirs could claim the proceeds from the friend.

definition: IRDAI's standardized depreciation rates applied to vehicle parts during motor insurance claims. Rates: Rubber/nylon/plastic parts — 50%, Fiberglass — 30%, Windshield glass — 0% (first claim, depreciation on subsequent), Wooden parts — 100%, Metal body — varies by vehicle age (0-15%). Zero depreciation add-on waives these deductions.
example: Your car's bumper (plastic, ₹15,000), headlight (glass, ₹8,000), and fender (metal, 2-year-old car) need replacement. Depreciation: bumper 50% (₹7,500 deducted), headlight glass 0% (₹0 deducted), fender metal 10% (₹1,000 deducted). Without zero dep: you pay ₹8,500 out of pocket. With zero dep: you pay ₹0.

definition: The depreciation rate applied to a vehicle's ex-showroom price to calculate the Insured Declared Value (IDV). IRDAI rates: less than 6 months: 5%, 6 months-1 year: 15%, 1-2 years: 20%, 2-3 years: 30%, 3-4 years: 40%, 4-5 years: 50%, beyond 5 years: by mutual agreement between insurer and policyholder.
example: Car ex-showroom price: ₹12 lakh. Age: 3 years (30% depreciation). IDV = ₹12L × (1 - 30%) = ₹8.4 lakh. This is the maximum the insurer will pay for total loss/theft. You can negotiate IDV ±10% within IRDAI guidelines. Higher IDV = higher premium but better payout.

definition: Mandatory personal accident insurance for the owner-driver of a motor vehicle, mandated by the Motor Vehicles Amendment Act 2019. Cover amount: ₹15 lakh for both private cars and two-wheelers. Cannot be opted out unless the owner-driver has an existing standalone PA policy of ₹15 lakh or more. Premium is regulated by IRDAI.
example: Your motor insurance renewal includes ₹750 for CPA cover (₹15 lakh for owner-driver). You already have a standalone PA policy of ₹50 lakh. You can request exemption from CPA by providing proof of existing PA cover, saving ₹750. Without proof, CPA is compulsory and cannot be removed.

definition: A motor insurance policy that covers both Own Damage (damage to your vehicle) and Third Party Liability (damage to others). Combines OD cover (voluntary, market-priced) + TP cover (mandatory, IRDAI-priced) + CPA cover in a single policy. The recommended choice for most vehicle owners — TP-only leaves your own vehicle uninsured.
example: For a ₹10 lakh car: TP premium: ₹3,416 + OD premium: ₹8,000 (with 35% NCB) + CPA: ₹750 = total comprehensive premium ₹12,166/year. This covers: damage to your car (₹8 lakh IDV), injury/death to others (unlimited TP liability), and your own accidental death (₹15 lakh). TP-only would cost ₹3,416 but leave your ₹8 lakh car uninsured.

definition: An own damage-only motor policy covering damage to the policyholder's vehicle, without Third Party liability. Available since 2019 when IRDAI allowed OD and TP to be purchased separately. Allows the policyholder to buy OD from the insurer offering the best price/service while TP stays with the cheapest option or existing insurer.
example: Your 3-year TP policy (₹10,248 for 3 years) is still active. For OD cover, you buy a standalone OD policy from a different insurer offering better NCB transfer and lower premium: ₹7,500/year. Total: you have OD from Insurer A + TP from Insurer B = comprehensive coverage at an optimized cost.

definition: The legal liability of a vehicle owner for injury, death, or property damage caused to third parties (other road users, pedestrians, property) by their vehicle. Liability is unlimited under Indian motor law. TP insurance is mandatory for every vehicle on Indian roads. Driving without TP insurance is punishable with fine and imprisonment.
example: Your car hits a pedestrian who is hospitalized for 3 months and incurs ₹15 lakh in medical expenses + ₹5 lakh lost income. The Motor Accident Claims Tribunal (MACT) awards ₹25 lakh compensation. Your TP insurer pays the entire ₹25 lakh. Without TP insurance, you would pay ₹25 lakh from your own pocket + face criminal prosecution.

definition: A quasi-judicial body established under the Motor Vehicles Act 1988 to adjudicate motor accident compensation claims. Victims of motor accidents (or their families) file claims before MACT for compensation covering medical expenses, loss of income, disability, and pain and suffering. The insurer provides legal defense and pays awarded compensation.
example: A pedestrian is permanently disabled after being hit by an insured car. They file a MACT claim seeking ₹1 crore compensation. MACT awards ₹60 lakh based on the victim's age, income, disability percentage, and future medical costs. The motor insurer pays ₹60 lakh to the victim on behalf of the vehicle owner.

definition: The three-tier consumer dispute resolution system — District Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission (up to ₹1 crore), State Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission (₹1-10 crore), and National Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission (above ₹10 crore). Used for insurance claim disputes after exhausting insurer grievance and ombudsman processes.
example: Your ₹3 lakh health claim was rejected by the insurer and the Ombudsman. You file a complaint at the District Consumer Forum with all documents (₹500 filing fee). The forum examines the case and orders the insurer to pay ₹3 lakh + ₹50,000 compensation for deficiency of service + 9% interest from the claim date.

definition: IRDAI-mandated standard health insurance product that every health and general insurer must offer with identical features. Sum insured: ₹1-5 lakh. Features: no room rent limit, 30-day initial waiting, 48-month PED waiting, 5% copay for 60+ years, AYUSH cover, 141+ day care procedures. Premium varies by insurer. Enables true apple-to-apple comparison.
example: Star Health's Arogya Sanjeevani: ₹5 lakh SI for a 35-year-old = ₹7,500/year. HDFC ERGO's version: ₹7,200/year. Bajaj Allianz: ₹6,900/year. Same product, same features — compare only on price, claim settlement, and network hospitals. Ideal as a starter plan for those new to health insurance.

definition: IRDAI-mandated standard term insurance plan that every life insurer must offer with identical features. Sum assured: ₹5-25 lakh. Term: 5-40 years. Entry age: 18-65. Maturity age: max 70. No riders, no return of premium, no survival benefit. Pure protection at standardized terms. Premium varies by insurer. Designed for affordable basic life cover.
example: LIC's Saral Jeevan Bima: ₹25 lakh cover, 30-year term, age 30 = approximately ₹5,000/year. SBI Life's version: ₹4,800/year. Max Life: ₹4,500/year. For those who need basic life cover and find online term plans confusing, Saral Jeevan Bima offers a simple, standardized option.

definition: IRDAI-mandated standard immediate annuity plan that every life insurer must offer. Minimum purchase price: ₹1,000. Two annuity options: life annuity with 100% return of purchase price to nominee on death. Provides simple, transparent pension with guaranteed rates. Enables easy comparison across insurers.
example: You invest ₹30 lakh in LIC's Saral Pension at age 60. Annuity rate: 6.5%. Monthly pension: ₹16,250. On your death, nominee receives ₹30 lakh (100% of purchase price). SBI Life's version may offer 6.8% = ₹17,000/month. Compare rates across all insurers for the best deal on an identical product.

definition: Failure to disclose material facts (health conditions, smoking, occupation hazard, existing insurance) in the proposal form. Non-disclosure is the leading cause of claim rejection in India (35% of all rejections). Insurers can void the policy during the contestability period if non-disclosure is proven. After moratorium/Section 45 period, only fraud overrides this.
example: You do not disclose diabetes when buying health insurance. Two years later, you are hospitalized for diabetic ketoacidosis. The insurer investigates, finds your diabetes diagnosis predated the policy, and rejects the claim for non-disclosure. Had you disclosed diabetes, the policy would have covered it after the PED waiting period.

definition: The application form for insurance where the applicant provides personal details, health history, lifestyle habits, occupation, and financial information. Answers must be truthful and complete — the principle of 'utmost good faith' (uberrimae fidei) applies. Incorrect or incomplete information can lead to claim rejection or policy cancellation.
example: The health insurance proposal form asks about pre-existing conditions, surgeries in last 5 years, current medications, smoking/alcohol habits, and family medical history. Answering 'No' to smoking when you are a smoker constitutes material non-disclosure — claims related to respiratory or cardiac conditions can be rejected.

definition: The insurer's process of evaluating an applicant's risk profile to decide whether to accept, reject, or modify the terms of insurance. For health: medical history, age, BMI, smoking, family history. For life: same + income, occupation. For motor: vehicle type, age, location. Underwriting determines if you get standard rates, loaded premium, or exclusions.
example: A 45-year-old smoker with BMI 32 and family history of heart disease applies for term insurance. Underwriting outcome: accepted with 25% premium loading (₹15,000/year instead of ₹12,000) and a heart condition exclusion for the first 2 years. Non-smoker of same age: standard rates ₹12,000 with no exclusions.

definition: An additional charge on the standard premium due to higher risk factors identified during underwriting. Common reasons: pre-existing conditions (diabetes, hypertension), smoking, high BMI, hazardous occupation, or adverse family history. Loading is expressed as a percentage of base premium (e.g., 20% loading on a ₹10,000 premium = ₹12,000 actual premium).
example: Standard health premium for a 40-year-old: ₹15,000/year. You have controlled diabetes — insurer applies 30% loading: ₹15,000 × 1.3 = ₹19,500/year. Some insurers offer loading review after 3-5 claim-free years, potentially reducing the loading if the condition remains controlled.

definition: Another term for the free look period — the window after receiving a policy document during which the policyholder can cancel and receive a full refund with minimal deductions. In India: 15 days for offline purchases, 30 days for online/distance purchases. The policyholder can cancel for ANY reason during this period.
example: You buy a ULIP online and receive documents on March 1. During the 30-day cooling-off period, you research and discover the ULIP has higher charges than a mutual fund. You cancel on March 20 under the cooling-off provision. You receive a full refund minus stamp duty and pro-rata mortality charge (approximately ₹500).

definition: Goods and Services Tax levied on insurance premiums at 18% for all insurance products. For health insurance: 18% GST on the full premium (no input tax credit for individuals). For life insurance: 18% on first year premium, 18% on renewal for ULIPs; for traditional plans, 4.5% on first year and 2.25% on renewal premiums (after excluding savings component).
example: Health premium: ₹15,000 + 18% GST (₹2,700) = ₹17,700. The ₹2,700 GST is also eligible for Section 80D deduction. Term insurance: ₹12,000 + 18% GST (₹2,160) = ₹14,160. Motor TP: GST included in IRDAI-mandated rates. GST makes insurance 18% more expensive — a significant cost for consumers.

definition: Denial of an insurance claim by the insurer. Common reasons: non-disclosure of pre-existing disease (35%), waiting period not completed (25%), excluded treatment (20%), incomplete documentation (15%), policy lapsed (5%). Policyholder can appeal: insurer grievance cell → IGMS → Ombudsman → Consumer Court.
example: Your health claim for ₹2 lakh is rejected citing 'pre-existing condition not disclosed.' Appeal steps: (1) Request written rejection reason, (2) Submit counter-evidence to insurer's grievance cell, (3) If no resolution in 15 days, file on IGMS, (4) If IGMS fails, approach Insurance Ombudsman. 60% of ombudsman appeals are decided in favor of the policyholder.

definition: The two claim settlement methods in health insurance. Cashless: hospital bills the insurer directly at network hospitals; patient pays only non-covered items. Reimbursement: patient pays the hospital first, then submits documents for reimbursement. Cashless is faster and avoids cash flow burden; reimbursement offers freedom to choose any hospital.
example: Cashless at network hospital: you pay ₹5,000 (non-payable items), insurer pays ₹2.95 lakh directly to hospital. Reimbursement at non-network: you pay ₹3 lakh upfront, submit documents, wait 15-30 days, receive ₹2.85 lakh (after deducting ₹15,000 non-payable). Cashless saves ₹2.95 lakh in immediate cash flow.

definition: An increase in sum insured earned for each claim-free year, without any increase in premium. Different from motor NCB (which reduces premium). Health NCB typically adds 10-50% of base SI per year, up to 50-100% maximum. Some policies offer NCB as premium discount instead. NCB may reduce partially (not fully reset) on making a claim.
example: Your ₹10 lakh policy earns 20% NCB per claim-free year. After 3 years: SI = ₹16 lakh (₹10L + 60% NCB). You make a claim in year 4 — NCB reduces by 20% to 40%. SI becomes ₹14 lakh. Compare with motor NCB which resets to 0% on any claim. Health NCB is more policyholder-friendly.

definition: When porting a health insurance policy from one insurer to another, the new insurer must give credit for waiting periods already served with the previous insurer. If you completed 2 years of PED waiting with Insurer A, Insurer B must credit those 2 years — you only need to wait 2 more years (of a total 4-year PED waiting).
example: You have been with Insurer A for 3 years (of 4-year PED waiting). You port to Insurer B. Insurer B credits 3 years of PED waiting — only 1 year remains. Initial waiting period (30 days) and specific disease waiting (2 years) credits also transfer if already completed. Without portability rights, you would restart all waiting periods from zero.

definition: The guaranteed amount payable by the life insurer on death of the insured or on maturity of the policy. Different from sum insured (health) — sum assured does not reduce with claims. For term plans, only death benefit = sum assured. For endowments, maturity benefit = sum assured + bonuses. IRDAI mandates minimum sum assured = 10× annual premium for tax benefits.
example: A ₹1 crore term plan: sum assured is ₹1 crore. If the policyholder dies anytime during the policy term, nominee receives ₹1 crore. For an endowment: SA ₹10 lakh + bonuses ₹6 lakh = maturity ₹16 lakh, death benefit ₹16 lakh (SA + vested bonuses).

definition: The IRDAI-mandated maximum time for insurers to settle claims. Cashless: pre-authorization within 1 hour (emergency) or 3 hours (planned). Reimbursement: within 30 days of receiving all documents. Life death claims: within 30 days of receiving all documents. If the insurer requires investigation, additional 90 days. Interest payable on delays.
example: You submit a reimbursement claim on March 1 with all documents. IRDAI mandate: insurer must settle by March 31 (30 days). If settled on April 15 (15 days late), the insurer must pay interest at bank rate + 2% (approx 8.5%) for 15 days on the claim amount. For a ₹5 lakh claim: ₹5L × 8.5% × 15/365 = ₹1,747 interest.

definition: The formal process for resolving policyholder complaints against insurers. Hierarchy: (1) Insurer's customer service → (2) Insurer's Grievance Officer (respond within 15 days) → (3) IGMS portal → (4) Insurance Ombudsman (claims up to ₹50 lakh) → (5) Consumer Court. Each escalation level has defined timelines.
example: Your claim is wrongly rejected. Step 1: Call insurer — no resolution. Step 2: Email Grievance Officer — 15 days, still denied. Step 3: File on IGMS (igms.irda.gov.in) — insurer must respond within 15 days. Step 4: Approach Ombudsman (free, no lawyer needed). Step 5: If still unsatisfied, file at Consumer Court (₹500 fee, court decides in 3-6 months).

definition: Insurance purchased by an insurance company to protect itself against large losses. The primary insurer transfers part of its risk to a reinsurer (like GIC Re, Swiss Re, Munich Re). This is why insurers can offer large sum insured amounts — they share the risk with reinsurers. GIC Re is India's sole domestic reinsurer.
example: An insurer writes a ₹5 crore health policy. To protect against a single large claim, the insurer retains ₹1 crore risk and transfers ₹4 crore to GIC Re through a reinsurance treaty. If a ₹5 crore claim occurs, the insurer pays ₹1 crore and GIC Re pays ₹4 crore. The policyholder is unaffected — they receive the full ₹5 crore.

definition: Low-cost insurance products designed for economically weaker sections, as mandated by IRDAI. Maximum sum insured: ₹2 lakh (life) and ₹1 lakh (general). Premium: very low (₹100-500/year). Simplified documentation and claims process. Every insurer must allocate a portion of business to micro insurance. Often distributed through microfinance institutions and SHGs.
example: A daily wage worker buys micro insurance: ₹1 lakh life cover for ₹200/year and ₹50,000 health cover for ₹300/year. When hospitalized for dengue, the micro health policy covers the ₹15,000 hospital bill — saving the worker from potentially devastating out-of-pocket expense that could push the family into poverty.

definition: A health insurance policy purchased for 2-3 years in a single transaction, locking in the current premium rate and avoiding annual renewal hassles. Offers a 5-10% discount over single-year premiums. Protects against premium hikes during the multi-year period. IRDAI allows multi-year policies since 2022.
example: Single-year health premium: ₹15,000. A 3-year multi-year policy costs ₹42,750 (₹14,250/year — 5% discount). You save ₹2,250 over 3 years AND lock in the rate. If the insurer increases premium by 15% in year 2, you are unaffected. Multi-year also avoids the risk of forgetting to renew.

definition: The premium payable at the time of renewing an insurance policy for the next policy year. Renewal premium may differ from the first-year premium due to: age-band changes, premium revisions by the insurer, loading/discount adjustments, and NCB/cumulative bonus effects. Timely renewal is critical to avoid lapses and restart of waiting periods.
example: Your health insurance renewal: Last year's premium ₹12,000. This year: premium ₹14,000 (₹1,500 age-band increase + ₹1,000 insurer revision - ₹500 NCB discount). If you do not renew within 30 days of due date, the policy lapses. A new policy means fresh waiting periods — 30 days initial + 4 years PED.

definition: The age brackets used by health insurers to determine premium rates. Common bands: 18-25, 26-30, 31-35, 36-40, 41-45, 46-50, 51-55, 56-60, 61-65, 65+. Premium increases when you cross into a higher age band. Some insurers use 5-year bands, others use 1-year bands. The age-band jump can cause 15-30% premium increase.
example: Your health premium at age 34 (in 31-35 band): ₹12,000/year. At age 36 (crossing to 36-40 band): ₹15,000/year — a 25% jump from age-band change alone. Buying a multi-year policy before crossing the band saves the difference for 2-3 years. Factor in age-band jumps when planning long-term insurance costs.

definition: A provision ensuring the policyholder's right to renew their health insurance policy regardless of claims made, health deterioration, or age advancement. IRDAI mandates that all individual health policies must be renewable for life — the insurer cannot refuse renewal because of claims history or worsening health.
example: You have been claiming ₹5-8 lakh every year for dialysis treatment. Despite heavy claims, the insurer MUST renew your policy — they cannot refuse renewal. The premium may increase (age-band, general revision), but coverage cannot be denied. This is your legal right under IRDAI's guaranteed renewal regulation.

definition: Health insurance designed for individuals aged 60 and above. Features higher premiums (2-4x of young adults for the same SI), mandatory copay (10-20% in many plans), limited SI options, and simplified underwriting. Some plans cover specific conditions common in elderly: cataracts, joint replacements, cardiac procedures. Preventive health checkup often included.
example: A 65-year-old couple: Star Health Senior Citizens Red Carpet, ₹10 lakh SI, premium ₹42,000/year with 20% copay. A claim of ₹5 lakh for knee replacement: insurer pays ₹4 lakh (80%), you pay ₹1 lakh (20% copay). 80D deduction: full ₹42,000 eligible (₹50,000 limit for senior citizens).

definition: Quality certification for hospitals by the National Accreditation Board for Hospitals and Healthcare Providers (a constituent board of the Quality Council of India). NABH-accredited hospitals meet defined quality standards for patient care, safety, and infrastructure. Many insurers offer higher cashless limits and fewer restrictions at NABH hospitals.
example: Your health policy provides cashless with 0% copay at NABH-accredited hospitals, but 10% copay at non-NABH hospitals. A ₹3 lakh claim at a NABH hospital: insurer pays ₹3 lakh. Same claim at a non-NABH hospital: insurer pays ₹2.7 lakh, you pay ₹30,000 copay. Always prefer NABH-accredited hospitals.

definition: A fixed daily cash allowance paid for each day of hospitalization, regardless of the actual hospital bill. Provides money for incidental expenses (family meals, travel, attendant costs) not covered by the main health policy. Available as a standalone policy or rider. Typically ₹500-5,000 per day.
example: You have a hospital cash benefit of ₹2,000/day. You are hospitalized for 10 days for pneumonia. Hospital cash pays ₹20,000 (10 × ₹2,000) regardless of your hospital bill. This is in ADDITION to your main health policy paying the actual hospital bill. Use the ₹20,000 for lost income, family expenses, and recovery costs.

definition: Intensive Care Unit charges — significantly higher than regular ward rates (3-10x), covering specialized monitoring equipment, ventilator support, dedicated nursing, and continuous medical supervision. Most health policies cover ICU charges without sub-limits. Some policies cap ICU at 2x the room rent limit. ICU costs: ₹15,000-₹1 lakh/day depending on the hospital.
example: Your health policy has a room rent limit of ₹5,000/day. ICU rate is ₹40,000/day. If the policy caps ICU at 2x room rent (₹10,000/day), proportional deduction applies — the insurer covers only 25% (₹10,000/₹40,000) of all associated charges. A policy with no room rent limit avoids this issue entirely.

definition: Coverage for ambulance charges incurred to transport the patient to the hospital in an emergency. Most health policies include ambulance cover with a sub-limit of ₹2,000-₹10,000 per hospitalization. Does not cover non-emergency ambulance transfers. Air ambulance is covered separately under select plans with higher sub-limits.
example: You suffer a heart attack and are rushed to the hospital by ambulance. Ambulance cost: ₹5,000. Your policy covers ambulance up to ₹3,000. Insurer pays ₹3,000; you pay ₹2,000. For a comprehensive plan with ambulance cover of ₹10,000, the full ₹5,000 would be covered.

definition: A cost-sharing arrangement where the policyholder pays a fixed percentage of covered expenses after the deductible is met. Similar to copay in practice, though technically co-insurance applies after the deductible while copay applies to the total claim. In India, the terms copay and co-insurance are often used interchangeably.
example: Your super top-up has ₹5 lakh deductible and 10% co-insurance. Hospital bill: ₹12 lakh. After deductible: ₹7 lakh eligible. Co-insurance: you pay 10% of ₹7L = ₹70,000. Insurer pays ₹6.3 lakh. Total your cost: ₹5 lakh (deductible, covered by base policy) + ₹70,000 = ₹5.7 lakh.

definition: The risk that having insurance encourages the insured to take more risks or make fraudulent claims, since the financial consequences are borne by the insurer. Insurance companies use deductibles, copays, NCB, and claim investigation to mitigate moral hazard. Over-insurance also creates moral hazard.
example: A person with comprehensive motor insurance may drive more carelessly (moral hazard) since repairs are covered. Deductibles (₹1,000-₹15,000) ensure the policyholder has 'skin in the game.' NCB (up to 50% discount) rewards careful driving. Copay in health insurance discourages unnecessary hospitalization.

definition: The tendency of high-risk individuals to be more likely to purchase insurance, while low-risk individuals opt out — creating an imbalanced risk pool. Insurers combat this through underwriting (medical tests, health declarations), waiting periods, and risk-based pricing. If unchecked, adverse selection can make insurance unprofitable and premiums unaffordable.
example: If health insurance were sold without medical underwriting or waiting periods, people would buy it only when they know they need surgery — creating adverse selection. Waiting periods (30-day initial, 2-year specific, 4-year PED) prevent this by requiring commitment before coverage activates.

definition: The fundamental legal principle requiring both parties in an insurance contract to deal with complete honesty and transparency. The insured must disclose ALL material facts; the insurer must clearly explain all terms, conditions, and exclusions. Breach by either party can void the contract. This principle underpins all insurance law in India.
example: You must disclose your diabetes diagnosis (utmost good faith). The insurer must clearly explain that claims related to diabetes are not covered for 4 years. If you hide diabetes: your claim can be rejected. If the insurer hides exclusions in fine print: the claim must be honored or the policyholder can seek remedy.

definition: The legal requirement that the policyholder must have a financial interest in the life or property being insured — meaning they would suffer a financial loss if the insured event occurs. Without insurable interest, an insurance contract is void. In life insurance: self, spouse, children, business partners have automatic insurable interest.
example: You can insure your own life, your spouse's life, or your business partner's life (insurable interest exists). You cannot insure a stranger's life — no insurable interest. For property: you can insure your car, home, or rented property contents. A neighbor cannot insure your car since they have no financial interest in it.

definition: The primary or dominant cause of a loss, used to determine whether a claim is covered. In insurance, the claim is payable only if the proximate cause of the loss is a covered peril. If the proximate cause is excluded, the claim is rejected — even if intermediate causes are covered.
example: A man with pre-existing diabetes is hospitalized for a fracture from falling off stairs. Proximate cause: the fall (accident — covered from day 1). The diabetes is incidental, not the proximate cause. The insurer must pay the claim. If instead, the hospitalization was for diabetic ketoacidosis, the proximate cause is diabetes (PED — may be within waiting period).

definition: The distribution of insurance products through bank branches. Banks act as corporate agents for insurance companies, selling life, health, and general insurance to their banking customers. Bancassurance accounts for 30-40% of life insurance distribution in India. Banks can tie up with up to 3 life, 3 general, and 3 health insurers.
example: When you visit SBI for a home loan, the SBI branch manager offers SBI Life term insurance and SBI General health insurance (bancassurance). Convenience: you buy insurance while completing banking formalities. Premium is debited from your SBI account. Compare bancassurance prices with online — online is often 30-40% cheaper for the same product.

definition: An IRDAI-licensed entity that provides an online platform comparing insurance products from multiple insurers, helping consumers make informed choices. Major aggregators in India: Policybazaar, InsuranceDekho, Turtlemint. Aggregators cannot advise — they can only display and compare. Revenue model: commission from insurers on policies sold.
example: You visit Policybazaar to compare health insurance. It shows 15 plans from 10 insurers with features, premiums, and claim settlement ratios side by side. You select Niva Bupa ReAssure 2.0 and buy it through the platform. Policybazaar earns 15-20% commission from Niva Bupa. Your premium is the same as buying directly from Niva Bupa.

definition: An IRDAI-licensed intermediary who represents the policyholder (not the insurer) and provides advisory services across products from multiple insurers. Unlike agents (who represent one insurer), brokers can recommend the best product from the entire market. Brokers are legally obligated to act in the client's best interest.
example: An insurance broker analyzes your family's needs, existing policies, and budget. They recommend: ₹1.5 crore HDFC Life term (best premium), Niva Bupa ReAssure health (best features), Bajaj comprehensive motor (best claims). A single agent could only sell their company's products — the broker gives market-wide best options.

definition: An IRDAI-licensed individual authorized to sell insurance products of the insurer(s) they represent. Individual agents can represent one life insurer and one general insurer. Corporate agents (banks, NBFCs) can represent up to 3 insurers per category. Agents earn commission: 15-35% in first year (life), 15% (health), 15% (motor).
example: An LIC agent can sell only LIC products — they cannot offer HDFC Life or ICICI Pru alternatives. If LIC's product is not the best for you, the agent has a conflict of interest. First-year commission on an endowment: 25-35% of premium. On a ₹50,000 premium: ₹12,500-₹17,500 commission — explaining why agents push high-premium products.

definition: Insurance covering financial losses from cyber attacks, data breaches, identity theft, online fraud, social media hacking, and digital risks. An emerging product in India covering: unauthorized transactions, IT forensic costs, cyber extortion, legal liability from data breaches, and restoration costs. Available for individuals and businesses.
example: Your email is hacked and ₹2 lakh is stolen via unauthorized UPI transactions. Cyber insurance covers: ₹2 lakh financial loss + ₹30,000 forensic investigation + ₹15,000 legal expenses. Premium: ₹2,000-5,000/year for ₹5-10 lakh cover. Essential in India where UPI fraud and phishing attacks are rising rapidly.

definition: Two different metrics often confused. Claim Settlement Ratio (CSR): number of claims settled / number of claims received (measures claim acceptance). Incurred Claim Ratio (ICR): claims paid / premiums earned (measures profitability). High CSR (95%+) means the insurer pays most claims. High ICR (80%+) means most premium goes to claims, not profit.
example: Insurer A: CSR 98% (pays 98 of 100 claims — excellent), ICR 68% (keeps 32% for expenses/profit — normal). Insurer B: CSR 85% (rejects 15 claims out of 100 — concerning), ICR 90% (pays more in claims than it should — may raise premiums soon). Choose insurer A — better CSR despite lower ICR.

definition: Deliberately making false or misleading claims, providing fabricated documents, or misrepresenting facts to obtain insurance benefits illegally. Types: staged accidents (motor), inflated bills (health), false death claims (life), multiple claims for same loss. IRDAI mandates anti-fraud frameworks. Penalties: claim rejection, policy cancellation, criminal prosecution.
example: Common fraud types in India: (1) Inflating hospital bills to claim higher amount, (2) Getting hospitalized for conditions that do not require admission, (3) Staging motor accidents, (4) False death certificates for life claims. Insurers use AI-based fraud detection, cross-referencing claims databases, and investigative teams.

definition: A feature in some health insurance policies where certain waiting periods are waived — usually the specific disease waiting period (Exclusion List II). Some insurers waive specific disease waiting for policies with higher sum insured or premium. Portability regulations also provide effective waiting period waivers through credit transfer.
example: A premium health plan offers 'zero waiting period for Exclusion List II diseases' for SI above ₹25 lakh. You buy a ₹50 lakh policy — cataract, hernia, knee replacement are covered from day 1 (normally 2-year wait). Initial waiting (30 days) and PED waiting (4 years) still apply. Premium is 20% higher but no specific disease waiting.

definition: A fixed lump sum paid to the policyholder after a prolonged hospitalization (typically 10+ consecutive days) to cover recovery-related expenses not covered by the main policy — such as nutritional supplements, home nursing, physiotherapy, and income loss during recovery. Typically ₹5,000-₹25,000 per eligible hospitalization.
example: You are hospitalized for 15 days after a major surgery. Your policy's convalescence benefit pays ₹15,000 as a lump sum (since hospitalization exceeded 10 days). This is in addition to the full hospital bill covered by your main policy. Use it for home recovery expenses, special diet, and physiotherapy.

definition: Unplanned hospitalization due to a medical emergency requiring immediate treatment — accident, heart attack, stroke, acute appendicitis, etc. Emergency admissions do not require pre-authorization for cashless claims — the insurer must process retrospective authorization within 24 hours. No 24-hour minimum stay requirement for emergency admissions.
example: You have a heart attack and are rushed to the nearest hospital (not in your insurer's network). After stabilization, you inform the insurer. For emergencies, IRDAI mandates that insurers must provide cashless at non-network hospitals too, or process reimbursement on priority. The 'planned admission' pre-auth rule does not apply to emergencies.

definition: A reduced benefit period applied to new members joining a group insurance scheme midway through the policy year. During the lien period (typically 90 days), only accident-related claims are covered; illness-related claims are excluded. This prevents adverse selection where people join group insurance only when they anticipate medical expenses.
example: You join a new company on June 1 with group health insurance. Lien period: 90 days (June 1 to August 31). If you are hospitalized for dengue on July 15, the claim is rejected (illness during lien period). If hospitalized for an accident on July 15, the claim is covered. After September 1, all hospitalizations are covered.

definition: The minimum mandatory motor insurance covering your liability to third parties (other people, their property). Does NOT cover damage to your own vehicle. Third party liability is unlimited under Indian law. TP-only policy is the cheapest motor insurance option and the bare minimum legal requirement. Driving without TP insurance is illegal: fine up to ₹2,000 and/or 3 months imprisonment.
example: A TP-only policy for a car costs ₹3,416/year (IRDAI-mandated rate). If you hit a pedestrian, the insurer covers all compensation (potentially ₹1 crore+). But if your own car is damaged in the accident, there is zero coverage — you pay the entire repair cost. Comprehensive policy (OD + TP) is always recommended.

definition: The unique registration number assigned by IRDAI to every licensed insurer, broker, agent, TPA, and insurance web aggregator in India. Verifiable on IRDAI's website. Before buying any insurance product, verify the insurer's IRDAI registration number to ensure they are a legitimate, regulated entity.
example: Star Health's IRDAI registration: 129. HDFC Life: 101. LIC: 512. Policybazaar (web aggregator): IRDAI/WBA/27/16. Before purchasing from any insurer or platform, verify their registration at www.irdai.gov.in. Unregistered entities selling insurance are illegal — report them to IRDAI.

definition: EAZR AI's automated fraud detection system that identifies potentially fraudulent insurance practices targeting consumers: mis-selling, churning, inflated charges, unauthorized policy changes, and fake insurance schemes. Uses pattern recognition across policy documents, premium comparisons, and regulatory database checks to protect users.
example: A user uploads a policy that an agent sold as 'high-return investment.' EAZR's fraud detection flags: (1) IRR is only 4.5% vs agent's claimed 12%, (2) Premium is 35% of sum assured (high commission product), (3) Multiple policy replacements in 2 years (churning pattern). EAZR alerts the user and provides IGMS complaint template.

definition: The ratio of total insurance premiums collected to the country's GDP, measuring how widely insurance is adopted. India's insurance penetration: approximately 4% (life 3.2% + non-life 1%). Compare: global average 7%, US 12%, UK 10%. India's low penetration represents both a massive protection gap for consumers and a significant growth opportunity.
example: India's insurance penetration of 4% means ₹4 of every ₹100 of GDP goes to insurance premiums. With 140 crore population and rising incomes, even reaching 6% penetration would double the insurance market. EAZR's mission: increase insurance penetration by making insurance understandable, accessible, and affordable for every Indian consumer.
source: IRDAI Annual Report / Swiss Re Sigma
